meta.title: A Commentary Critical, Experimental, and Practical on the Old and New Testaments
meta.author: Robert Jamieson, Andrew R. Fausset, and David Brown
meta.tradition_notes: Jamieson was a Free Church of Scotland minister; Fausset an Anglican evangelical; Brown a Free Church of Scotland professor. The commentary reflects broadly Reformed evangelical scholarship. Interdenominational in spirit despite the Presbyterian institutional affiliations.

provenance.source_edition: HelloAO Bible API local download - A Commentary Critical, Experimental, and Practical on the Old and New Testaments (PDM 1.0 (https://creativecommons.org/publicdomain/mark/1.0/))
provenance.notes: Sourced from local copy of HelloAO Bible API data. Commentary licensed PDM 1.0 (public domain). BSB verse text from HelloAO BSB translation (CC0). Processed 31 chapters of Proverbs.

data.verse_text: These are the proverbs of Solomon son of David, king of Israel,
data.commentary_text: After the title the writer defines the design and nature of the instructions of the book. He paternally invites attention to those instructions and warns his readers against the enticements of the wicked. In a beautiful personification, wisdom is then introduced in a most solemn and impressive manner, publicly inviting men to receive its teachings, warning those who reject, and encouraging those who accept, the proffered instructions. (Pro. 1:1-33)

data.verse_text: for gaining wisdom and discipline, for comprehending words of insight,
data.commentary_text:
To know . . . instruction--literally, "for knowing," that is, such is the design of these writings. 

wisdom--or the use of the best means for the best ends, is generally employed in this book for true piety. 

instruction--discipline, by which men are trained. 

to perceive--literally, "for perceiving," the design (as above) 

understanding--that is, words which enable one to discern good and evil.

data.verse_text: and for receiving instruction in wise living and in righteousness, justice, and equity.
data.commentary_text:
To receive . . . of wisdom--For receiving that discipline which discretion imparts. The Hebrew for "wisdom" differs from that of Pro 1:2, and denotes rather discreet counsel. Compare the opposite traits of the fool (Pro 16:22). 

justice . . . equity--all the attributes of one upright in all his relations to God and man.

data.verse_text: To impart prudence to the simple and knowledge and discretion to the young,
data.commentary_text:
simple--one easily led to good or evil; so the parallel. 

young man--one inexperienced. 

subtilty--or prudence (Pro 3:21; Pro 5:21). 

discretion--literally, "device," both qualities, either good or bad, according to their use. Here good, as they imply wariness by which to escape evil and find good.

data.verse_text: let the wise listen and gain instruction, and the discerning acquire wise counsel
data.commentary_text:
Such writings the wise, who pursue right ends by right means, will value. 

learning--not the act, but matter of it. 

wise counsels--or the art and principles of governing.

data.verse_text: by understanding the proverbs and parables, the sayings and riddles of the wise.
data.commentary_text:
To understand--so as to . . . such will be the result. 

interpretation--(Compare Margin). 

words of the wise--(Compare Pro 1:2). 

dark sayings--(Compare Psa 49:4; Joh 16:25; and see Introduction, Part I).

data.verse_text: The fear of the LORD is the beginning of knowledge, but fools despise wisdom and discipline.
data.commentary_text:
The fear of the Lord--the principle of true piety (compare Pro 2:5; Pro 14:26-27; Job 28:28; Psa 34:11; Psa 111:10; Act 9:31). 

beginning--first part, foundation. 

fools--the stupid and indifferent to God's character and government; hence the wicked.

data.verse_text: Listen, my son, to your father’s instruction, and do not forsake the teaching of your mother.
data.commentary_text: My son--This paternal form denotes a tender regard for the reader. Filial sentiments rank next to piety towards God, and ensure most distinguished rewards (compare Pro 6:20; Eph 6:2-3).

data.verse_text: For they are a garland of grace on your head and a pendant around your neck.
data.commentary_text: On the figures of Pro 1:9, compare Gen 41:42; Sol 1:10; Sol 4:9.

data.verse_text: My son, if sinners entice you, do not yield to them.
data.commentary_text:
A solemn warning against temptation. 

entice--literally, "open the way." 

consent . . . not--Sin is in consenting or yielding to temptation, not in being tempted.

data.verse_text: If they say, “Come along, let us lie in wait for blood, let us ambush the innocent without cause, let us swallow them alive like Sheol, and whole like those descending into the Pit. We will find all manner of precious goods; we will fill our houses with plunder. Throw in your lot with us; let us all share one purse”—
data.commentary_text:
Murder and robbery are given as specific illustrations. 

lay wait . . . lurk privily--express an effort and hope for successful concealment. 

swallow . . . grave--utterly destroy the victim and traces of the crime (Num 16:33; Psa 55:15). Abundant rewards of villainy are promised as the fruits of this easy and safe course.

data.verse_text: my son, do not walk the road with them or set foot upon their path. For their feet run to evil, and they are swift to shed blood.
data.commentary_text: The society of the wicked (way or path) is dangerous. Avoid the beginnings of sin (Pro 4:14; Psa 1:1; Psa 119:101).

data.verse_text: How futile it is to spread the net where any bird can see it! But they lie in wait for their own blood; they ambush their own lives. Such is the fate of all who are greedy, whose unjust gain takes the lives of its possessors.
data.commentary_text: Men warned ought to escape danger as birds instinctively avoid visibly spread nets. But stupid sinners rush to their own ruin (Psa 9:16), and, greedy of gain, succeed in the very schemes which destroy them (Ti1 6:10), not only failing to catch others, but procuring their own destruction.

data.verse_text: Wisdom calls out in the street, she lifts her voice in the square;
data.commentary_text:
Some interpreters regard this address as the language of the Son of God under the name of Wisdom (compare Luk 11:49). Others think that wisdom, as the divine attribute specially employed in acts of counsel and admonition, is here personified, and represents God. In either case the address is a most solemn and divine admonition, whose matter and spirit are eminently evangelical and impressive (see on Pro 8:1). 

Wisdom--literally, "Wisdoms," the plural used either because of the unusual sense, or as indicative of the great excellency of wisdom (compare Pro 9:1). 

streets--or most public places, not secretly.

data.verse_text: in the main concourse she cries aloud, at the city gates she makes her speech:
data.commentary_text: The publicity further indicated by terms designating places of most common resort.

data.verse_text: “How long, O simple ones, will you love your simple ways? How long will scoffers delight in their scorn and fools hate knowledge?
data.commentary_text:
simple ones--(Compare Pro 1:4). 

simplicity--implying ignorance. 

scorners-- (Psa 1:1) --who despise, as well as reject, truth. 

fools--Though a different word is used from that of Pro 1:7, yet it is of the same meaning.

data.verse_text: If you had repented at my rebuke, then surely I would have poured out my spirit on you; I would have made my words known to you.
data.commentary_text:
reproof--implying conviction deserving it (compare Joh 16:8, Margin). 

pour out--abundantly impart. 

my spirit--whether of wisdom personified, or of Christ, a divine agent.

data.verse_text: Because you refused my call, and no one took my outstretched hand,
data.commentary_text: stretched . . . hand--Earnestness, especially in beseeching, is denoted by the figure (compare Job 11:13; Psa 68:31; Psa 88:9).

data.verse_text: because you neglected all my counsel, and wanted none of my correction,
data.commentary_text:
set at naught--rejected as of no value. 

would none of--literally, "were not willing or inclined to it."

data.verse_text: in turn I will mock your calamity; I will sneer when terror strikes you,
data.commentary_text: In their extreme distress He will not only refuse help, but aggravate it by derision.

data.verse_text: when your dread comes like a storm, and your destruction like a whirlwind, when distress and anguish overwhelm you.
data.commentary_text:
fear--the object of it. 

desolation--literally, "a tumultuous noise," denoting their utter confusion. 

destruction--or calamity (Pro 1:26) compared to a whirlwind, as to fatal rapidity. 

distress-- (Psa 4:1; Psa 44:11). 

anguish--a state of inextricable oppression, the deepest despair.

data.verse_text: Then they will call on me, but I will not answer; they will earnestly seek me, but will not find me.
data.commentary_text: Now no prayers or most diligent seeking will avail (Pro 8:17).

data.verse_text: For they hated knowledge and chose not to fear the LORD. They accepted none of my counsel; they despised all my reproof.
data.commentary_text: The sinner's infatuated rejection brings his ruin.

data.verse_text: So they will eat the fruit of their own way, and be filled with their own devices.
data.commentary_text:
fruit . . . way--result of conduct (Isa 3:10; Eze 11:21; Rom 6:21; Gal 6:7-8). 

be filled--even to repletion (Psa 123:4).

data.verse_text: For the waywardness of the simple will slay them, and the complacency of fools will destroy them.
data.commentary_text:
turning away--that is, from the call of Pro 1:23. 

simple--as in Pro 1:22. 

prosperity--quiet, implying indifference.

data.verse_text: But whoever listens to me will dwell in safety, secure from the fear of evil.”
data.commentary_text:
dwell safely--literally, "in confidence" (Deu 12:10). 

be quiet--or at ease, in real prosperity. 

from fear--without fear.




Next: Proverbs Chapter 2

data.verse_text: My son, if you accept my words and hide my commandments within you,
data.commentary_text:
Men are invited to seek wisdom because it teaches those principles by which they may obtain God's guidance and avoid the society and influence of the wicked, whose pernicious courses are described. (Pro. 2:1-22)

Diligence in hearing and praying for instruction must be used to secure the great principle of godliness, the fear of God. 

hide . . . with thee--lay up in store (compare Pro 7:1).

data.verse_text: if you incline your ear to wisdom and direct your heart to understanding,
data.commentary_text:
Listen attentively and reflect seriously (Pro 1:24; Psa 130:2). 

understanding--right perception of truth.

data.verse_text: if you truly call out to insight and lift your voice to understanding,
data.commentary_text:
Yea, if--literally, "When if," that is, in such a case. 

knowledge--or, "discrimination." 

understanding--as in Pro 2:2.

data.verse_text: if you seek it like silver and search it out like hidden treasure,
data.commentary_text: There must be earnest prayer and effort.

data.verse_text: then you will discern the fear of the LORD and discover the knowledge of God.
data.commentary_text:
understand--or, "perceive intelligently." 

find--obtain.

data.verse_text: For the LORD gives wisdom; from His mouth come knowledge and understanding.
data.commentary_text:
For--God is ready (Jam 1:5; Jam 4:8). 

out of his mouth--by revelation from Him.

data.verse_text: He stores up sound wisdom for the upright; He is a shield to those who walk with integrity,
data.commentary_text:
sound wisdom--literally, "substance," opposed to what is fictitious. According to the context, this may be assistance, as here corresponding with 

buckler--or safety, or wisdom, which procures it (compare Pro 3:21; Pro 8:14; Pro 18:1; Job 6:13; Job 12:13). 

layeth up--provides, ever ready.

data.verse_text: to guard the paths of justice and protect the way of His saints.
data.commentary_text:
keepeth . . . way--God defends the right way, and those in it. 

saints--objects of favor (compare Psa 4:3, &c.). He guides and guards them.

data.verse_text: Then you will discern righteousness and justice and equity—every good path.
data.commentary_text:
Then--emphatic, in such a case. 

righteousness . . . path--all parts of duty to God and man.

data.verse_text: For wisdom will enter your heart, and knowledge will delight your soul. Discretion will watch over you, and understanding will guard you,
data.commentary_text: Idea of Pro 2:9, amplified; on terms, compare Pro 2:2 and Pro 2:4.

data.verse_text: to deliver you from the way of evil, from the man who speaks perversity,
data.commentary_text:
To deliver--as from great danger (Pro 6:5). 

way . . . man-- (Psa 1:1). 

froward things--perversity (Pro 6:14; Pro 23:23), what is opposed to truth.

data.verse_text: from those who leave the straight paths to walk in the ways of darkness,
data.commentary_text:
paths of uprightness--or, "plainness." 

walk--habitually act;

data.verse_text: from those who enjoy doing evil and rejoice in the twistedness of evil,
data.commentary_text:
and that with pleasure, in ignorance of good and pursuit of evil. 

frowardness--Not only their own perversity, but that of others is their delight. They love most the worst things.

data.verse_text: whose paths are crooked and whose ways are devious.
data.commentary_text:
crooked--tortuous, unprincipled. 

froward--literally, (they) are going back, not only aside from right, but opposite to it.

data.verse_text: It will rescue you from the forbidden woman, from the stranger with seductive words
data.commentary_text:
Deliverance from another danger. 

the strange woman--This term is often used for harlot, or loose woman (Jdg 11:1-2), married (Pro 7:5, Pro 7:19) or not (Kg1 11:1), so called, because such were, perhaps at first, foreigners, though "strange" may also denote whatever is opposed to right or proper, as "strange fire" (Num 3:4); "strange incense" (Exo 30:9). 

flattereth--literally, "smooths." 

her words-- (Psa 5:9).

data.verse_text: who abandons the partner of her youth and forgets the covenant of her God.
data.commentary_text:
guide . . . youth--lawful husband (Jer 3:4). 

covenant . . . God--of marriage made in God's name.

data.verse_text: For her house sinks down to death, and her tracks to the departed spirits.
data.commentary_text:
inclineth--sinks down (compare Num 13:31). 

the dead--or shades of the departed (Psa 88:10).

data.verse_text: None who go to her return or negotiate the paths of life.
data.commentary_text:
that is, such as remain impenitent (compare Ecc 7:26). 

paths of life-- (Psa 16:11), opposed to paths unto the dead.

data.verse_text: So you will follow in the ways of the good, and keep to the paths of the righteous.
data.commentary_text: That . . . way of good--that is, Such is the object of these warnings.

data.verse_text: For the upright will inhabit the land, and the blameless will remain in it;
data.commentary_text: (Compare Psa 37:3, Psa 37:9, Psa 37:22, Psa 37:27).

data.verse_text: but the wicked will be cut off from the land, and the unfaithful will be uprooted.
data.commentary_text:
transgressors--or impious rebels (compare Jer 9:2). 

rooted out--utterly destroyed, as trees plucked up by the roots.




Next: Proverbs Chapter 3

data.verse_text: My son, do not forget my teaching, but let your heart keep my commandments;
data.commentary_text:
The study of truth commended. God must be feared, honored, and trusted, and filial submission, under chastisement, exhibited. The excellence of wisdom urged and illustrated by its place in the divine counsels. Piety enforced by a contrast of the destiny of the righteous and the wicked. (Pro. 3:1-35)

law [and] commandments--all divine instructions (see on Psa 119:1 and Psa 119:4). 

let thine heart keep--or sincerely observe (Pro 4:13; Pro 5:2).

data.verse_text: for they will add length to your days, years and peace to your life.
data.commentary_text:
length . . . life--often promised as blessings (Psa 21:4; Psa 91:16). 

peace--includes prosperity (Psa 125:5). 

add--abound to thee.

data.verse_text: Never let loving devotion or faithfulness leave you; bind them around your neck, write them on the tablet of your heart.
data.commentary_text:
mercy and truth--God's faithfulness to His promises is often expressed by these terms (Psa 25:10; Psa 57:3). As attributes of men, they express integrity in a wide sense (Pro 16:6; Pro 20:28). 

bind . . . write . . . heart--outwardly adorn and inwardly govern motives.

data.verse_text: Then you will find favor and high regard in the sight of God and man.
data.commentary_text:
favour--grace, amiability (Pro 22:11; Psa 45:2); united with this, 

a good understanding--(Compare Margin), a discrimination, which secures success. 

in the sight . . . man--such as God and man approve.

data.verse_text: Trust in the LORD with all your heart, and lean not on your own understanding;
data.commentary_text: Trust . . . heart--This is the center and marrow of true wisdom (Pro 22:19; Pro 28:25). The positive duty has its corresponding negation in the admonition against self-confidence.

data.verse_text: in all your ways acknowledge Him, and He will make your paths straight.
data.commentary_text:
ways-- (Psa 1:1). 

acknowledge--by seeking His wise aid (Pro 16:3; Psa 37:5; Jer 9:23-24). 

direct--literally, "make plain" (compare Heb 12:13).

data.verse_text: Be not wise in your own eyes; fear the LORD and turn away from evil.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 27:2; Rom 12:16). 

fear . . . evil--reverentially regarding His law.

data.verse_text: This will bring healing to your body and refreshment to your bones.
data.commentary_text:
It--This conduct. 

health--(Compare Margin). 

to thy navel--for all the organs of nourishment. 

marrow--(Compare Margin). 

bones--frame of body. True piety promotes bodily health.

data.verse_text: Honor the LORD with your wealth and with the firstfruits of all your harvest;
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 11:25; Exo 23:19; Deu 18:4; Isa 32:8; Co2 9:13).

data.verse_text: then your barns will be filled with plenty, and your vats will overflow with new wine.
data.commentary_text: presses--or wine fats (Joe 2:24; Joe 3:13).

data.verse_text: My son, do not reject the discipline of the LORD, and do not loathe His rebuke;
data.commentary_text: The true intent of afflictions considered; they do not contradict the assertion of the blessed state of the pious (Job 5:17; Heb 12:5-6).

data.verse_text: for the LORD disciplines the one He loves, as does a father the son in whom he delights.
data.commentary_text: he delighteth--or receiveth as denoting reconciliation regarding the offense which produced chastisement.

data.verse_text: Blessed is the man who finds wisdom, the man who acquires understanding,
data.commentary_text:
findeth--literally, "reaches," or "obtains by seeking." 

getteth--literally, "draws out," as metals by digging.

data.verse_text: for she is more profitable than silver, and her gain is better than fine gold.
data.commentary_text:
The figure of Pro 3:13 carried out. 

it--that is, wisdom. 

merchandise of silver--acquisition by trading. 

fine gold--dug gold, solid as a nugget.

data.verse_text: She is more precious than rubies; nothing you desire compares with her.
data.commentary_text: rubies--gems, or pearls.

data.verse_text: Long life is in her right hand; in her left hand are riches and honor.
data.commentary_text: Wisdom personified as bringing the best blessings (compare Mat 6:33; Ti1 4:8).

data.verse_text: All her ways are pleasant, and all her paths are peaceful.
data.commentary_text: Her ways--such as she directs us to take.

data.verse_text: She is a tree of life to those who embrace her, and those who lay hold of her are blessed.
data.commentary_text:
Wisdom allegorized as 

a tree of life-- (Gen 2:9; Gen 3:22) whose fruit preserves life, gives all that makes living a blessing.

data.verse_text: The LORD founded the earth by wisdom and established the heavens by understanding. By His knowledge the watery depths were broken open, and the clouds dripped with dew.
data.commentary_text: The place of wisdom in the economy of creation and providence commends it to men, who, in proportion to their finite powers, may possess this invaluable attribute, and are thus encouraged by the divine example of its use to seek its possession.

data.verse_text: My son, do not lose sight of this: Preserve sound judgment and discernment.
data.commentary_text:
sound wisdom--(compare Pro 2:7). 

let . . . eyes--that is, these words of instruction.

data.verse_text: They will be life to your soul and adornment to your neck. Then you will go on your way in safety, and your foot will not stumble. When you lie down, you will not be afraid; when you rest, your sleep will be sweet.
data.commentary_text: assign reasons in their value for happiness and ornament, guidance and support in dangers, both when waking and sleeping.

data.verse_text: Do not fear sudden danger or the ruin that overtakes the wicked,
data.commentary_text:
Be not--or, "You shall not be." 

sudden fear--what causes it (Pro 1:27), any unlooked-for evil (Psa 46:3; Psa 91:12; Pe1 3:14). 

desolation-- (Pro 1:27).

data.verse_text: for the LORD will be your confidence and will keep your foot from the snare.
data.commentary_text:
The reason; such as are objects of God's favor. 

be thy confidence--literally, "in thy confidence," in the source of thy strength (compare Nah 3:9, for the same construction, Hebrew).

data.verse_text: Do not withhold good from the deserving when it is within your power to act. Do not tell your neighbor, “Come back tomorrow and I will provide”— when you already have the means.
data.commentary_text: Promptly fulfil all obligations both of justice and charity (compare Jam 2:15-16).

data.verse_text: Do not devise evil against your neighbor, for he trustfully dwells beside you. Do not accuse a man without cause, when he has done you no harm.
data.commentary_text: Do not abuse confidence and avoid litigation.

data.verse_text: Do not envy a violent man or choose any of his ways;
data.commentary_text: oppressor--or man of mischief. The destiny of successful evildoers warns against desiring their lot (Psa 37:1-2, Psa 37:35-36).

data.verse_text: for the LORD detests the perverse, but He is a friend to the upright.
data.commentary_text:
Reasons for the warning. 

froward-- (Pro 2:15). 

secret . . . righteous--in their communion (Amo 3:7).

data.verse_text: The curse of the LORD is on the house of the wicked, but He blesses the home of the righteous.
data.commentary_text: curse . . . wicked--It abides with them, and will be manifested.

data.verse_text: He mocks the mockers, but gives grace to the humble.
data.commentary_text: The retribution of sinners, as in Psa 18:26.

data.verse_text: The wise will inherit honor, but fools are held up to shame.
data.commentary_text:
inherit--as a portion. 

shame--or disgrace, as opposed to honor. 

promotion--(Compare Margin); as honor for well-doing makes men conspicuous, so fools are signalized by disgrace.




Next: Proverbs Chapter 4

data.verse_text: Listen, my sons, to a father’s instruction; pay attention and gain understanding. For I give you sound teaching; do not abandon my directive.
data.commentary_text:
To an earnest call for attention to his teachings, the writer adds a commendation of wisdom, preceded and enforced by the counsels of his father and teacher. To this he adds a caution (against the devices of the wicked), and a series of exhortations to docility, integrity, and uprightness. (Pro. 4:1-27)

(Compare Pro 1:8). 

to know--in order to know. 

doctrine--the matter of learning (Pro 1:5), such as he had received (Lam 3:1).

data.verse_text: When I was a son to my father, tender and the only child of my mother,
data.commentary_text:
father's son--emphatic, a son specially regarded, and so called tender, as an object of special care (compare Ch1 22:7; Ch1 29:1); an idea further expressed by 

only beloved--or, "as an only son" (Gen 22:2), though he had brothers (see on Ch1 3:5).

data.verse_text: he taught me and said, “Let your heart lay hold of my words; keep my commands and you will live.
data.commentary_text:
He taught--or directed me. 

retain--as well as receive. 

keep . . . and live--observe, that you may live (Pro 7:2).

data.verse_text: Get wisdom, get understanding; do not forget my words or turn from them.
data.commentary_text:
Get--as a possession not to be given up. 

neither decline--that is, from obeying my word.

data.verse_text: Do not forsake wisdom, and she will preserve you; love her, and she will guard you.
data.commentary_text: Not only accept but love wisdom, who will keep thee from evil, and evil from thee.

data.verse_text: Wisdom is supreme; so acquire wisdom. And whatever you may acquire, gain understanding.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Job 28:28). 

getting--or possession; a desire for wisdom is wise.

data.verse_text: Prize her, and she will exalt you; if you embrace her, she will honor you.
data.commentary_text:
As you highly esteem her, she will raise you to honor. 

embrace her--with fond affection.

data.verse_text: She will set a garland of grace on your head; she will present you with a crown of beauty.”
data.commentary_text:
ornament--such as the chaplet or wreath of conquerors. 

deliver--(Compare Gen 14:20). The allusion to a shield, contained in the Hebrew, suggests protection as well as honor (compare Pro 4:6).

data.verse_text: Listen, my son, and receive my words, and the years of your life will be many.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 2:1; Pro 3:2).

data.verse_text: I will guide you in the way of wisdom; I will lead you on straight paths. When you walk, your steps will not be impeded; when you run, you will not stumble.
data.commentary_text:
way of wisdom--which it prescribes. 

led thee--literally, "caused thee to tread," as a path (Psa 107:7). 

not be straitened--have ample room (Psa 18:36).

data.verse_text: Hold on to instruction; do not let go. Guard it, for it is your life.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 3:18). The figure of laying hold with the hand suggests earnest effort.

data.verse_text: Do not set foot on the path of the wicked or walk in the way of evildoers. Avoid it; do not travel on it. Turn from it and pass on by.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Psa 1:1). Avoid all temptations to the beginning of evil.

data.verse_text: For they cannot sleep unless they do evil; they are deprived of slumber until they make someone fall. For they eat the bread of wickedness and drink the wine of violence.
data.commentary_text: The reason is found in the character of sinners, whose zeal to do evil is forcibly depicted (Pro 6:4; Psa 36:5). They live by flagrant vices (Pro 1:13). Some prefer to render, "Their bread is wickedness, their drink violence" (compare Job 15:16; Job 34:7).

data.verse_text: The path of the righteous is like the first gleam of dawn, shining brighter and brighter until midday. But the way of the wicked is like the darkest gloom; they do not know what makes them stumble.
data.commentary_text: As shining light increases from twilight to noonday splendor, so the course of the just increases in purity, but that of the wicked is as thickest darkness, in which one knows not on what he stumbles.

data.verse_text: My son, pay attention to my words; incline your ear to my sayings. Do not lose sight of them; keep them within your heart.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 4:10, Pro 4:13; Pro 3:8, &c.).

data.verse_text: For they are life to those who find them, and health to the whole body.
data.commentary_text: health . . . flesh--by preserving from vices destructive of health.

data.verse_text: Guard your heart with all diligence, for from it flow springs of life.
data.commentary_text: with all diligence--or, "above," or "more than all," custody (compare Margin), all that is kept (compare Eze 38:7), because the heart is the depository of all wisdom and the source of whatever affects life and character (Mat 12:35; Mat 15:19).

data.verse_text: Put away deception from your mouth; keep your lips from perverse speech.
data.commentary_text:
a froward mouth--that is, a mouth, or words of ill nature. The Hebrew word differs from that used (Pro 2:15; Pro 3:32). 

perverse--or, "quarreling." 

lips--or, "words."

data.verse_text: Let your eyes look forward; fix your gaze straight ahead.
data.commentary_text: Let . . . before thee--that is, pursue a sincere and direct purpose, avoiding temptations.

data.verse_text: Make a level path for your feet, and all your ways will be sure.
data.commentary_text: Ponder--Consider well; a wise course results from wise forethought.

data.verse_text: Do not swerve to the right or to the left; turn your feet away from evil.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 4:25). Avoid all by-paths of evil (Deu 2:27; Deu 17:11). A life of integrity requires attention to heart, speech, eyes, and conduct.




Next: Proverbs Chapter 5

data.verse_text: My son, pay attention to my wisdom; incline your ear to my insight,
data.commentary_text:
A warning against the seductive arts of wicked women, enforced by considering the advantages of chastity, and the miserable end of the wicked. (Pro. 5:1-23)

This connection of wisdom and understanding is frequent (Pro 2:2; Pro 3:7); the first denotes the use of wise means for wise ends; the other, the exercise of a proper discrimination in their discovery.

data.verse_text: that you may maintain discretion and your lips may preserve knowledge.
data.commentary_text:
regard--or, "observe." 

keep--preserve constantly.

data.verse_text: Though the lips of the forbidden woman drip honey and her speech is smoother than oil,
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 2:16). Her enticing promises are deceitful.

data.verse_text: in the end she is bitter as wormwood, sharp as a double-edged sword.
data.commentary_text: her end--literally, "her future," in sense of reward, what follows (compare Psa 37:37; Psa 73:17). Its nature is evinced by the use of figures, opposite those of Pro 5:3. The physical and moral suffering of the deluded profligate are notoriously terrible.

data.verse_text: Her feet go down to death; her steps lead straight to Sheol.
data.commentary_text: feet . . . , steps--that is, course of life ends in death.

data.verse_text: She does not consider the path of life; she does not know that her ways are unstable. So now, my sons, listen to me, and do not turn aside from the words of my mouth.
data.commentary_text: her ways . . . know--Some prefer, "that she may not ponder the path of life," &c.; but perhaps a better sense is, "her ways are varied, so as to prevent your knowledge of her true character, and so of true happiness."

data.verse_text: Keep your path far from her; do not go near the door of her house,
data.commentary_text: Avoid the slightest temptation.

data.verse_text: lest you concede your vigor to others, and your years to one who is cruel;
data.commentary_text:
thine honour--in whatever consisting, strength (Pro 3:13) or wealth. 

thy years--by cutting them off in dissipation. 

unto the cruel--for such the sensual are apt to become.

data.verse_text: lest strangers feast on your wealth, and your labors enrich the house of a foreigner.
data.commentary_text:
wealth--literally, "strength," or the result of it. 

labours--the fruit of thy painful exertions (Psa 127:2). There may be a reference to slavery, a commuted punishment for death due the adulterer (Deu 22:22).

data.verse_text: At the end of your life you will groan when your flesh and your body are spent,
data.commentary_text:
at the last--the end, or reward (compare Pro 5:4). 

mourn--roar in pain. 

flesh and . . . body--the whole person under incurable disease.

data.verse_text: and you will say, “How I hated discipline, and my heart despised reproof! I did not listen to the voice of my teachers or incline my ear to my mentors.
data.commentary_text: The ruined sinner vainly laments his neglect of warning and his sad fate in being brought to public disgrace.

data.verse_text: I am on the brink of utter ruin in the midst of the whole assembly.”
data.commentary_text: evil--for affliction, as in Gen 19:20; Gen 49:15.

data.verse_text: Drink water from your own cistern, and running water from your own well. Why should your springs flow in the streets, your streams of water in the public squares?
data.commentary_text: By figures, in which well, cistern, and fountain [Pro 5:15, Pro 5:18] represent the wife, and rivers of waters [Pro 5:16] the children, men are exhorted to constancy and satisfaction in lawful conjugal enjoyments. In Pro 5:16, fountains (in the plural) rather denote the produce or waters of a spring, literally, "what is from a spring," and corresponds with "rivers of waters."

data.verse_text: Let them be yours alone, never to be shared with strangers.
data.commentary_text: only thine own--harlots' children have no known father.

data.verse_text: May your fountain be blessed, and may you rejoice in the wife of your youth:
data.commentary_text: wife . . . youth--married in youth.

data.verse_text: A loving doe, a graceful fawn— may her breasts satisfy you always; may you be captivated by her love forever. Why be captivated, my son, by an adulteress, or embrace the bosom of a stranger?
data.commentary_text:
loving . . . roe--other figures for a wife from the well-known beauty of these animals. 

breasts--(Compare Sol 1:13; Eze 23:3, Eze 23:8). 

ravished--literally, "intoxicated," that is, fully satisfied.

data.verse_text: For a man’s ways are before the eyes of the LORD, and the LORD examines all his paths.
data.commentary_text: The reason, God's eye is on you,

data.verse_text: The iniquities of a wicked man entrap him; the cords of his sin entangle him.
data.commentary_text: and He will cause sin to bring its punishment.

data.verse_text: He dies for lack of discipline, led astray by his own great folly.
data.commentary_text:
without instruction--literally, "in want of instruction," having refused it (compare Job 13:18; Heb 11:24). 

go astray--literally, "be drunken." The word "ravished" (Pro 5:19) here denotes fulness of punishment.




Next: Proverbs Chapter 6

data.verse_text: My son, if you have put up security for your neighbor, if you have struck hands in pledge with a stranger, if you have been trapped by the words of your lips, ensnared by the words of your mouth,
data.commentary_text:
After admonitions against suretyship and sloth (compare Pro 6:6-8), the character and fate of the wicked generally are set forth, and the writer (Pro. 6:20-35) resumes the warnings against incontinence, pointing out its certain and terrible results. This train of thought seems to intimate the kindred of these vices. (Pro. 6:1-35)

if--The condition extends through both verses. 

be surety--art pledged. 

stricken . . . hand--bargained (compare Job 17:3). 

with a stranger--that is, for a friend (compare Pro 11:15; Pro 17:18).

data.verse_text: then do this, my son, to free yourself, for you have fallen into your neighbor’s hands: Go, humble yourself, and press your plea with your neighbor.
data.commentary_text:
come . . . friend--in his power. 

humble . . . sure thy friend--urge as a suppliant; that is, induce the friend to provide otherwise for his debt, or secure the surety.

data.verse_text: Allow no sleep to your eyes or slumber to your eyelids. Free yourself, like a gazelle from the hand of the hunter, like a bird from the snare of the fowler.
data.commentary_text: The danger requires promptness.

data.verse_text: Walk in the manner of the ant, O slacker; observe its ways and become wise. Without a commander, without an overseer or ruler, it prepares its provisions in summer; it gathers its food at harvest.
data.commentary_text: The improvident sluggards usually want sureties. Hence, such are advised to industry by the ant's example.

data.verse_text: How long will you lie there, O slacker? When will you get up from your sleep? A little sleep, a little slumber, a little folding of the hands to rest,
data.commentary_text: Their conduct graphically described;

data.verse_text: and poverty will come upon you like a robber, and need like a bandit.
data.commentary_text:
and the fruits of their self-indulgence and indolence presented. 

as . . . travelleth--literally, "one who walks backwards and forwards," that is, a highwayman. 

armed man--that is, one prepared to destroy.

data.verse_text: A worthless person, a wicked man, walks with a perverse mouth,
data.commentary_text: A naughty person--literally, "A man of Belial," or of worthlessness, that is, for good, and so depraved, or wicked (compare Sa1 25:25; Sa1 30:22, &c.). Idleness and vice are allied. Though indolent in acts, he actively and habitually (walketh) is ill-natured in speech (Pro 4:24).

data.verse_text: winking his eyes, speaking with his feet, and pointing with his fingers.
data.commentary_text: If, for fear of detection, he does not speak, he uses signs to carry on his intrigues. These signs are still so used in the East.

data.verse_text: With deceit in his heart he devises evil; he continually sows discord.
data.commentary_text:
Frowardness--as in Pro 2:14. 

deviseth--literally, "constructs, as an artisan." 

mischief--evil to others. 

discord--especially litigation. Cunning is the talent of the weak and lazy.

data.verse_text: Therefore calamity will come upon him suddenly; in an instant he will be shattered beyond recovery.
data.commentary_text:
Suddenness aggravates evil (compare Pro 6:11; Pro 29:1). 

calamity--literally, "a crushing weight." 

broken--shivered as a potter's vessel; utterly destroyed (Psa 2:9).

data.verse_text: There are six things that the LORD hates, seven that are detestable to Him:
data.commentary_text: six . . . seven--a mode of speaking to arrest attention (Pro 30:15, Pro 30:18; Job 5:19).

data.verse_text: haughty eyes, a lying tongue, hands that shed innocent blood, a heart that devises wicked schemes, feet that run swiftly to evil,
data.commentary_text: proud look--literally, "eyes of loftiness" (Psa 131:1). Eyes, tongue, &c., for persons.

data.verse_text: a false witness who gives false testimony, and one who stirs up discord among brothers.
data.commentary_text: speaketh--literally, "breathes out," habitually speaks (Psa 27:12; Act 9:1).

data.verse_text: My son, keep your father’s commandment, and do not forsake your mother’s teaching. Bind them always upon your heart; tie them around your neck.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 1:8; Pro 3:3, &c.).

data.verse_text: When you walk, they will guide you; when you lie down, they will watch over you; when you awake, they will speak to you.
data.commentary_text: it--(compare Pro 6:23); denotes the instruction of parents (Pro 6:20), to which all the qualities of a safe guide and guard and ready teacher are ascribed. It prevents the ingress of evil by supplying good thoughts, even in dreams (Pro 3:21-23; Psa 19:9; Pe2 1:19).

data.verse_text: For this commandment is a lamp, this teaching is a light, and the reproofs of discipline are the way to life,
data.commentary_text: reproofs-- (Pro 1:23) the convictions of error produced by instruction.

data.verse_text: to keep you from the evil woman, from the smooth tongue of the adulteress.
data.commentary_text: A specimen of its benefit. By appreciating truth, men are not affected by lying flattery.

data.verse_text: Do not lust in your heart for her beauty or let her captivate you with her eyes.
data.commentary_text:
One of the cautions of this instruction, avoid alluring beauty. 

take--or, "ensnare." 

eyelids--By painting the lashes, women enhanced beauty.

data.verse_text: For the levy of the prostitute is poverty, and the adulteress preys upon your very life.
data.commentary_text:
The supplied words give a better sense than the old version: "The price of a whore is a piece of bread." 

adulteress--(Compare Margin), which the parallel and context (Pro 6:29-35) sustain. Of similar results of this sin, compare Pro 5:9-12. 

will hunt--alluding to the snares spread by harlots (compare Pro 7:6-8). 

precious life--more valuable than all else.

data.verse_text: Can a man embrace fire and his clothes not be burned? Can a man walk on hot coals without scorching his feet? So is he who sleeps with another man’s wife; no one who touches her will go unpunished.
data.commentary_text: The guilt and danger most obvious.

data.verse_text: Men do not despise the thief if he steals to satisfy his hunger.
data.commentary_text: Such a thief is pitied, though heavily punished.

data.verse_text: Yet if caught, he must pay sevenfold; he must give up all the wealth of his house.
data.commentary_text: sevenfold--(compare Exo 22:1-4), for many, ample (compare Gen 4:24; Mat 18:21), even if all his wealth is taken.

data.verse_text: He who commits adultery lacks judgment; whoever does so destroys himself.
data.commentary_text: lacketh understanding--or, "heart"; destitute of moral principle and prudence.

data.verse_text: Wounds and dishonor will befall him, and his reproach will never be wiped away.
data.commentary_text:
dishonour--or, "shame," as well as hurt of body (Pro 3:35). 

reproach . . . away--No restitution will suffice;

data.verse_text: For jealousy enrages a husband, and he will show no mercy in the day of vengeance. He will not be appeased by any ransom, or persuaded by lavish gifts.
data.commentary_text:
nor any terms of reconciliation be admitted. 

regard--or, "accept" any ransom.




Next: Proverbs Chapter 7

data.verse_text: My son, keep my words and treasure my commandments within you.
data.commentary_text:
The subject continued, by a delineation of the arts of strange women, as a caution to the unwary. (Pro. 7:1-27)

Similar calls (Pro 3:1-3; Pro 4:10, &c.).

data.verse_text: Keep my commandments and live; guard my teachings as the apple of your eye.
data.commentary_text: apple . . . eye--pupil of eye, a custody (Pro 4:23) of special value.

data.verse_text: Tie them to your fingers; write them on the tablet of your heart. Say to wisdom, “You are my sister,” and call understanding your kinsman,
data.commentary_text: Bind . . . fingers--as inscriptions on rings.

data.verse_text: that they may keep you from the adulteress, from the stranger with seductive words.
data.commentary_text: The design of the teaching (compare Pro 2:16; Pro 6:24).

data.verse_text: For at the window of my house I looked through the lattice.
data.commentary_text:
For--or, "Since," introducing an example to illustrate the warning, which, whether a narrative or a parable, is equally pertinent. 

window--or, "opening" 

looked--literally, "watched earnestly" (Jdg 5:28). 

casement--or, "lattice."

data.verse_text: I saw among the simple, I noticed among the youths, a young man lacking judgment,
data.commentary_text:
simple--as in Pro 1:4. 

void of, &c.--(Compare Pro 6:32).

data.verse_text: crossing the street near her corner, strolling down the road to her house,
data.commentary_text:
her corner--where she was usually found. 

went . . . house--implying, perhaps, confidence in himself by his manner, as denoted in the word 

went--literally, "tread pompously."

data.verse_text: at twilight, as the day was fading into the dark of the night.
data.commentary_text:
The time, twilight, ending in darkness. 

black . . . night--literally, "pupil," or, "eye," that is, middle of night.

data.verse_text: Then a woman came out to meet him, with the attire of a harlot and cunning of heart.
data.commentary_text:
attire--that of harlots was sometimes peculiar. 

subtile--or, "wary," "cunning."

data.verse_text: She is loud and defiant; her feet do not remain at home. Now in the street, now in the squares, she lurks at every corner.
data.commentary_text:
loud--or, "noisy," "bustling." 

stubborn--not submissive. 

without . . . streets, . . . corner--(Compare Ti1 5:13; Tit 2:5).

data.verse_text: She seizes him and kisses him; she brazenly says to him: “I have made my peace offerings; today I have paid my vows. So I came out to meet you; I sought you, and I have found you.
data.commentary_text: The preparations for a feast do not necessarily imply peculiar religious professions. The offerer retained part of the victim for a feast (Lev 3:9, &c.). This feast she professes was prepared for him whom she boldly addresses as one sought specially to partake of it.

data.verse_text: I have decked my bed with coverings, with colored linen from Egypt.
data.commentary_text: my bed--or, "couch," adorned in the costliest manner.

data.verse_text: I have perfumed my bed with myrrh, with aloes, and with cinnamon.
data.commentary_text: bed--a place for sleeping.

data.verse_text: Come, let us take our fill of love till morning. Let us delight in loving caresses! For my husband is not at home; he has gone on a long journey.
data.commentary_text: There is no fear of discovery.

data.verse_text: He took with him a bag of money and will not return till the moon is full.”
data.commentary_text: the day appointed--perhaps, literally, "a full moon," that is, a fortnight's time (compare Pro 7:19).

data.verse_text: With her great persuasion she entices him; with her flattering lips she lures him.
data.commentary_text:
caused . . . yield--or, "inclines." 

flattering--(Compare Pro 5:3). 

forced him--by persuasion overcoming his scruples.

data.verse_text: He follows her on impulse, like an ox going to the slaughter, like a deer bounding into a trap,
data.commentary_text: straightway--quickly, either as ignorant of danger, or incapable of resistance.

data.verse_text: until an arrow pierces his liver, like a bird darting into a snare— not knowing it will cost him his life.
data.commentary_text: Till--He is now caught (Pro 6:26).

data.verse_text: Now, my sons, listen to me, and attend to the words of my mouth. Do not let your heart turn aside to her ways; do not stray into her paths.
data.commentary_text: The inferential admonition is followed (Pro 7:26-27), by a more general allegation of the evils of this vice.

data.verse_text: For she has brought many down to death; her slain are many in number. Her house is the road to Sheol, descending to the chambers of death.
data.commentary_text:
Even the mightiest fail to resist her deathly allurements.




Next: Proverbs Chapter 8

data.verse_text: Does not wisdom call out, and understanding raise her voice? On the heights overlooking the road, at the crossroads she takes her stand. Beside the gates to the city, at the entrances she cries out: “To you, O men, I call out, and my cry is to the sons of men.
data.commentary_text:
Contrasted with sensual allurements are the advantages of divine wisdom, which publicly invites men, offers the best principles of life, and the most valuable benefits resulting from receiving her counsels. Her relation to the divine plans and acts is introduced, as in Pro 3:19-20, though more fully, to commend her desirableness for men, and the whole is closed by an assurance that those finding her find God's favor, and those neglecting ruin themselves. Many regard the passage as a description of the Son of God by the title, Wisdom, which the older Jews used (and by which He is called in Luk 11:49), as Joh 1:1, &c., describes Him by that of Logos, the Word. But the passage may be taken as a personification of wisdom: for, (1) Though described as with God, wisdom is not asserted to be God. (2) The use of personal attributes is equally consistent with a personification, as with the description of a real person. (3) The personal pronouns used accord with the gender (feminine) of wisdom constantly, and are never changed to that of the person meant, as sometimes occurs in a corresponding use of spirit, which is neuter in Greek, but to which masculine pronouns are often applied (Joh 16:14), when the acts of the Holy Spirit are described. (4) Such a personification is agreeable to the style of this book (compare Pro 1:20; Pro 3:16-17; Pro 4:8; Pro 6:20-22; Pro 9:1-4), whereas no prophetical or other allusions to the Saviour or the new dispensation are found among the quotations of this book in the New Testament, and unless this be such, none exist. (5) Nothing is lost as to the importance of this passage, which still remains a most ornate and also solemn and impressive teaching of inspiration on the value of wisdom. (Pro. 8:1-36)

The publicity and universality of the call contrast with the secrecy and intrigues of the wicked (Pro 7:8, &c.).

data.verse_text: O simple ones, learn to be shrewd; O fools, gain understanding.
data.commentary_text:
wisdom--literally, "subtilty" in a good sense, or, "prudence." 

fools--as Pro 1:22.

data.verse_text: Listen, for I speak of noble things, and the opening of my lips will reveal right.
data.commentary_text:
excellent things--or, "plain," "manifest." 

opening . . . things--upright words.

data.verse_text: For my mouth will speak the truth, and wickedness is detestable to my lips.
data.commentary_text:
For . . . truth--literally, "My palate shall meditate," or (as Orientals did) "mutter," my thoughts expressed only to myself are truth. 

wickedness--specially falsehood, as opposed to truth.

data.verse_text: All the words of my mouth are righteous; none are crooked or perverse.
data.commentary_text:
in righteousness--or, "righteous" (Psa 9:8; Psa 11:7). 

froward--literally, "twisted," or contradictory, that is, to truth.

data.verse_text: They are all plain to the discerning, and upright to those who find knowledge.
data.commentary_text:
plain . . . understandeth--easily seen by those who apply their minds. 

that find--implying search.

data.verse_text: Receive my instruction instead of silver, and knowledge rather than pure gold.
data.commentary_text: not silver--preferable to it, so last clause implies comparison.

data.verse_text: For wisdom is more precious than rubies, and nothing you desire compares with her.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 3:14-15).

data.verse_text: I, wisdom, dwell together with prudence, and I find knowledge and discretion.
data.commentary_text:
prudence--as in Pro 8:5. The connection of "wisdom" and "prudence" is that of the dictates of sound wisdom and its application. 

find . . . inventions--or, "devices," "discreet ways" (Pro 1:4).

data.verse_text: To fear the LORD is to hate evil; I hate arrogant pride, evil conduct, and perverse speech.
data.commentary_text:
For such is the effect of the fear of God, by which hatred to evil preserves from it. 

froward mouth--or, "speech" (Pro 2:12; Pro 6:14).

data.verse_text: Counsel and sound judgment are mine; I have insight and strength.
By me kings reign, and rulers enact just laws; By me princes rule, and all nobles who govern justly.
data.commentary_text: of which a wisely conducted government is an example.

data.verse_text: I love those who love me, and those who seek me early shall find me.
data.commentary_text: early--or, "diligently," which may include the usual sense of early in life.

data.verse_text: With me are riches and honor, enduring wealth and righteousness.
data.commentary_text: durable riches . . . righteousness--Such are the "riches," enduring sources of happiness in moral possessions (compare Pro 3:16).

data.verse_text: My fruit is better than gold, pure gold, and my harvest surpasses choice silver.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 8:11; Pro 3:16).

data.verse_text: I walk in the way of righteousness, along the paths of justice, bestowing wealth on those who love me and making their treasuries full.
data.commentary_text: The courses in which wisdom leads conduct to a true present prosperity (Pro 23:5).

data.verse_text: The LORD created me as His first course, before His works of old.
data.commentary_text:
Strictly, God's attributes are part of Himself. Yet, to the poetical structure of the whole passage, this commendation of wisdom is entirely consonant. In order of time all His attributes are coincident and eternal as Himself. But to set forth the importance of wisdom as devising the products of benevolence and power, it is here assigned a precedence. As it has such in divine, so should it be desired in human, affairs (compare Pro 3:19). 

possessed--or, "created"; in either sense, the idea of precedence. 

in the beginning--or simply, "beginning," in apposition with "me." 

before . . . of old--preceding the most ancient deeds.

data.verse_text: From everlasting I was established, from the beginning, before the earth began.
data.commentary_text: I was set up--ordained, or inaugurated (Psa 2:6). The other terms carry out the idea of the earliest antiquity, and illustrate it by the details of creation [Pro 8:24-29].

data.verse_text: When there were no watery depths, I was brought forth, when no springs were overflowing with water.
data.commentary_text:
brought forth--(Compare Psa 90:2). 

abounding--or, "laden with water."

data.verse_text: Before the mountains were settled, before the hills, I was brought forth,
data.commentary_text: settled--that is, sunk in foundations.

data.verse_text: before He made the land or fields, or any of the dust of the earth.
data.commentary_text:
fields--or, "out places," "deserts," as opposite to (habitable) "world." 

highest part--or, "sum," all particles together,

data.verse_text: I was there when He established the heavens, when He inscribed a circle on the face of the deep,
data.commentary_text: when he set . . . depth--marked out the circle, according to the popular idea of the earth, as circular, surrounded by depths on which the visible concave heavens rested.

data.verse_text: when He established the clouds above, when the fountains of the deep gushed forth,
data.commentary_text: established . . . deep--that is, so as to sustain the waters above and repress those below the firmament (Gen 1:7-11; Job 26:8).

data.verse_text: when He set a boundary for the sea, so that the waters would not surpass His command, when He marked out the foundations of the earth.
data.commentary_text:
commandment--better, the shore, that is, of the sea. 

foundations--figuratively denotes the solid structure (Job 38:4; Psa 24:2).

data.verse_text: Then I was a skilled craftsman at His side, and His delight day by day, rejoicing always in His presence. I was rejoicing in His whole world, delighting together in the sons of men.
data.commentary_text: one brought up--an object of special and pleasing regard. The bestowal of wisdom on men is represented by its finding a delightful residence and pleasing God.

data.verse_text: Now therefore, my sons, listen to me, for blessed are those who keep my ways. Listen to instruction and be wise; do not ignore it.
data.commentary_text: Such an attribute men are urged to seek.

data.verse_text: Blessed is the man who listens to me, watching daily at my doors, waiting at the posts of my doorway.
data.commentary_text: watching . . . waiting--literally, "so as to watch"; wait, denoting a most sedulous attention.

data.verse_text: For whoever finds me finds life and obtains the favor of the LORD.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Luk 13:23-24).

data.verse_text: But he who fails to find me harms himself; all who hate me love death.”
data.commentary_text:
sinneth . . . me--or better, "missing me," as opposed to "finding" [Pro 8:35]. 

love death--act as if they did (compare Pro 17:9).




Next: Proverbs Chapter 9

data.verse_text: Wisdom has built her house; she has carved out her seven pillars.
data.commentary_text:
The commendation of wisdom is continued, under the figure of a liberal host, and its provisions under that of a feast (compare Luk 14:16-24). The character of those who are invited is followed by a contrasted description of the rejectors of good counsel; and with the invitations of wisdom are contrasted the allurement of the wicked woman. (Pro. 9:1-18)

house--(compare Pro 8:34). 

her--or, "its" (the house). 

seven pillars--the number seven for many, or a sufficiency (Pro 6:31).

data.verse_text: She has prepared her meat and mixed her wine; she has also set her table.
data.commentary_text:
mingled--to enhance the flavor (Pro 23:30; Isa 5:22). 

furnished--literally, "set out," "arranged."

data.verse_text: She has sent out her maidservants; she calls out from the heights of the city.
data.commentary_text:
maidens--servants to invite (compare Psa 68:11; Isa 40:9). 

highest places--ridges of heights, conspicuous places.

data.verse_text: “Whoever is simple, let him turn in here!” she says to him who lacks judgment. “Come, eat my bread and drink the wine I have mixed. Leave your folly behind, and you will live; walk in the way of understanding.”
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 1:4; Pro 6:32). Wisdom not only supplies right but forbids wrong principles.

data.verse_text: He who corrects a mocker brings shame on himself; he who rebukes a wicked man taints himself. Do not rebuke a mocker, or he will hate you; rebuke a wise man, and he will love you.
data.commentary_text:
shame--(Compare Pro 3:35). 

a blot--or, "stain on character." Both terms denote the evil done by others to one whose faithfulness secures a wise man's love.

data.verse_text: Instruct a wise man, and he will be wiser still; teach a righteous man, and he will increase his learning.
data.commentary_text: The more a wise man learns, the more he loves wisdom.

data.verse_text: The fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom, and knowledge of the Holy One is understanding.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 1:7). 

of the holy--literally, "holies," persons or things, or both. This knowledge gives right perception.

data.verse_text: For through wisdom your days will be multiplied, and years will be added to your life.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 3:16-18; Pro 4:10).

data.verse_text: If you are wise, you are wise to your own advantage; but if you scoff, you alone will bear the consequences.
data.commentary_text: You are mainly concerned in your own conduct.

data.verse_text: The woman named Folly is loud; she is naive and knows nothing.
data.commentary_text:
foolish woman--or literally, "woman of folly," specially manifested by such as are described. 

clamorous--or, "noisy" (Pro 7:11). 

knoweth nothing--literally, "knoweth not what," that is, is right and proper.

data.verse_text: She sits at the door of her house, on a seat in the heights of the city,
data.commentary_text: on a seat--literally, "throne," takes a prominent place, impudently and haughtily.

data.verse_text: calling out to those who pass by, who make their paths straight. “Whoever is simple, let him turn in here!” she says to him who lacks judgment.
data.commentary_text:
to allure those who are right-minded, and who are addressed as in Pro 9:4, as 

simple--that is, easily led (Pro 1:4) and unsettled, though willing to do right.

data.verse_text: “Stolen water is sweet, and bread eaten in secret is tasty!”
data.commentary_text: The language of a proverb, meaning that forbidden delights are sweet and pleasant, as fruits of risk and danger.

data.verse_text: But they do not know that the dead are there, that her guests are in the depths of Sheol.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 2:18-19; Pro 7:27).




Next: Proverbs Chapter 10

data.verse_text: The proverbs of Solomon: A wise son brings joy to his father, but a foolish son grief to his mother.
data.commentary_text:
Here begins the second part of the book, Pro. 10:1-22:16, which, with the third, Pro. 22:16-25:28, contains series of proverbs whose sense is complete in one or two verses, and which, having no logical connection, admit of no analysis. The parallelisms of Pro. 10:1-15:33 are mostly antithetic; and those of Pro. 16:1-22:16, synthetic. The evidences of art in the structure are very clear, and indicate, probably, a purpose of facilitating the labor of memorizing. (Pro. 10:1-32)

wise [and] foolish--as they follow or reject the precepts of wisdom. 

maketh . . . father--or, "gladdens a father." 

heaviness--or, "grief."

data.verse_text: Ill-gotten treasures profit nothing, but righteousness brings deliverance from death.
data.commentary_text:
Treasures . . . nothing--that is, Ill-gotten gains give no true happiness (compare Pro 4:17; Mat 6:19). 

righteousness--especially beneficence (Psa 112:9). 

death--the greatest of all evils.

data.verse_text: The LORD does not let the righteous go hungry, but He denies the craving of the wicked.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Psa 37:16-20). The last clause is better: "He will repel the greedy desires of the wicked."

data.verse_text: Idle hands make one poor, but diligent hands bring wealth.
data.commentary_text:
slack--literally, "deceitful," failing of its purpose (compare Hos 7:16). 

maketh rich--(compare Pro 10:22).

data.verse_text: He who gathers in summer is a wise son, but he who sleeps during harvest is a disgraceful son.
data.commentary_text:
son--as Pro 1:8, Pro 1:10, and often. 

sleepeth--in indolence, and not for rest. 

causeth shame--literally, "is base" (compare Pro 14:35; Pro 17:2).

data.verse_text: Blessings are on the head of the righteous, but the mouth of the wicked conceals violence.
data.commentary_text: Blessings--literally, "Praises." The last clause is better: "The mouth of the wicked covereth (or concealeth) violence (or mischievous devices)" to be executed in due time (Psa 5:9; Psa 10:7; Rom 3:14), and hence has no praises (compare Pro 10:11).

data.verse_text: The memory of the righteous is a blessing, but the name of the wicked will rot.
data.commentary_text:
blessed--literally, "for a blessing," or praise. 

shall rot--literally, "be worm-eaten," useless and disgusting.

data.verse_text: A wise heart will receive commandments, but foolish lips will come to ruin.
data.commentary_text:
wise, &c.--(compare Pro 9:8-9, Pro 9:16), opposed to 

prating fool--or, "fool of lips of wicked language." 

fall--headlong, suddenly.

data.verse_text: He who walks in integrity walks securely, but he who perverts his ways will be found out.
data.commentary_text:
perverteth his ways--acts deceitfully. 

known--discovered and punished.

data.verse_text: He who winks the eye causes grief, and foolish lips will come to ruin.
data.commentary_text: Two vices contrasted; hypocrisy, or insinuating evil against one (Pro 6:13; Psa 35:19), and rashness of speech. In each case, the results are on the evildoers.

data.verse_text: The mouth of the righteous is a fountain of life, but the mouth of the wicked conceals violence.
data.commentary_text: a well--or, "source" of good to himself and others (Joh 7:37-38). On last clause, see on Pro 10:6.

data.verse_text: Hatred stirs up dissension, but love covers all transgressions.
data.commentary_text:
strifes--or, "litigations." 

covereth--by forgiveness and forbearance.

data.verse_text: Wisdom is found on the lips of the discerning, but a rod is for the back of him who lacks judgment.
data.commentary_text:
In the lips . . . found--hence, not beaten, as the wicked-speaking fool. 

void of understanding-- (Pro 6:32; Pro 7:7).

data.verse_text: The wise store up knowledge, but the mouth of the fool invites destruction.
data.commentary_text:
lay up knowledge--that is, as treasures for good use. 

mouth . . . destruction--or, "as to the mouth," &c., destruction is near; they expose themselves to evil by prating.

data.verse_text: The wealth of the rich man is his fortified city, but poverty is the ruin of the poor.
data.commentary_text: Both by trusting in "uncertain riches" (Ti1 6:17), or by the evils of poverty (Pro 30:9), men, not fearing God, fall into dangers.

data.verse_text: The labor of the righteous leads to life, but the gain of the wicked brings punishment.
data.commentary_text: The industry of the righteous is alone truly successful, while the earnings of the wicked tempt and lead to sin.

data.verse_text: Whoever heeds instruction is on the path to life, but he who ignores reproof goes astray.
data.commentary_text:
keepeth--observes (Pro 3:18; Pro 4:22). 

refuseth--or, "turns from reproof," which might direct him aright.

data.verse_text: The one who conceals hatred has lying lips, and whoever spreads slander is a fool.
data.commentary_text: Both vices must one day be known and punished, and hence their folly.

data.verse_text: When words are many, sin is unavoidable, but he who restrains his lips is wise.
data.commentary_text: Much speech involves risk of sin; hence the wisdom of restraining the tongue (Psa 39:1; Jam 1:26).

data.verse_text: The tongue of the righteous is choice silver, but the heart of the wicked has little worth.
data.commentary_text: Right speech is the fruit of a good heart, but the wicked show theirs to be useless.

data.verse_text: The lips of the righteous feed many, but fools die for lack of judgment.
data.commentary_text: Fools not only fail to benefit others, as do the righteous, but procure their own ruin (compare Pro 10:11, Pro 10:17; Hos 4:6).

data.verse_text: The blessing of the LORD enriches, and He adds no sorrow to it.
data.commentary_text: it maketh, &c.--"it" is emphatic. Riches from God are without the sorrow of ill-gotten wealth (compare Ecc 2:21-23; Ti1 6:9-10, Ti1 6:17).

data.verse_text: The fool delights in shameful conduct, but a man of understanding has wisdom.
data.commentary_text: Sin is the pleasure of the wicked; wisdom that of the good.

data.verse_text: What the wicked man dreads will overtake him, but the desire of the righteous will be granted.
data.commentary_text: it--the very thing. The wicked get dreaded evil; the righteous, desired good.

data.verse_text: When the whirlwind passes, the wicked are no more, but the righteous are secure forever.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Psa 1:4; Psa 37:9-10, Psa 37:36). 

righteous . . . foundation--well laid and firm (Mat 7:24-25).

data.verse_text: Like vinegar to the teeth and smoke to the eyes, so is the slacker to those who send him.
data.commentary_text: that is, causes vexation.

data.verse_text: The fear of the LORD prolongs life, but the years of the wicked will be cut short.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 9:11; Psa 55:23).

data.verse_text: The hope of the righteous is joy, but the expectations of the wicked will perish.
data.commentary_text:
gladness--in confidence of realizing it. 

expectation . . . perish--in disappointment.

data.verse_text: The way of the LORD is a refuge to the upright, but destruction awaits those who do evil.
data.commentary_text: The way, &c.--that is, God's providence sustains the righteous and overthrows the wicked (Hos 14:9).

data.verse_text: The righteous will never be shaken, but the wicked will not inhabit the land.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 12:3; Psa 37:9-11; Psa 102:28). 

earth--or, "land of promise."

data.verse_text: The mouth of the righteous brings forth wisdom, but a perverse tongue will be cut out.
data.commentary_text:
bringeth forth--literally, "germinates" as a plant. 

froward--(Compare Pro 2:12, Pro 2:14). 

cut off--as an unproductive plant.

data.verse_text: The lips of the righteous know what is fitting, but the mouth of the wicked is perverse.
data.commentary_text:
know--regard and provide for (Psa 1:6). 

frowardness--all kinds of deceit and ill-nature. The word is plural.




Next: Proverbs Chapter 11

data.verse_text: Dishonest scales are an abomination to the LORD, but an accurate weight is His delight.
data.commentary_text:
(Pro. 11:1-31)

(Compare Margin). The Hebrews used stones for weights. 

just--complete in measure.

data.verse_text: When pride comes, disgrace follows, but with humility comes wisdom.
data.commentary_text: Self-conceit is unteachable; the humble grow wise (compare Pro 16:18; Pro 18:12).

data.verse_text: The integrity of the upright guides them, but the perversity of the faithless destroys them.
data.commentary_text:
guide--to lead, as a shepherd (Pro 6:7; Psa 78:52). 

perverseness--ill-nature. 

destroy--with violence.

data.verse_text: Riches are worthless in the day of wrath, but righteousness brings deliverance from death.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 10:2). 

wrath--that is, of God.

data.verse_text: The righteousness of the blameless directs their path, but the wicked fall by their own wickedness.
data.commentary_text: direct--or, "make plain"; wicked ways are not plain (Pro 13:17).

data.verse_text: The righteousness of the upright delivers them, but the faithless are trapped by their own desires.
data.commentary_text: deliver them--that is, from evil, which the wicked suffer by their own doings (Pro 5:22; Psa 9:16).

data.verse_text: When the wicked man dies, his hope perishes, and the hope of his strength vanishes.
data.commentary_text:
expectation . . . perish--for death cuts short all his plans (Luk 16:25). 

hope of unjust--better, "hope of wealth," or "power" (compare Isa 40:29, Hebrew). This gives an advance on the sentiment of the first clause. Even hopes of gain die with him.

data.verse_text: The righteous man is delivered from trouble; in his place the wicked man goes in.
data.commentary_text: Perhaps the trouble prepared by the wicked, and which he inherits (compare Pro 11:6).

data.verse_text: With his mouth the ungodly man destroys his neighbor, but through knowledge the righteous are rescued.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Psa 35:16; Dan 11:32). The just is saved by superior discernment.

data.verse_text: When the righteous thrive, the city rejoices, and when the wicked perish, there are shouts of joy.
data.commentary_text: The last may be a reason for the first. Together, they set forth the relative moral worth of good and bad men.

data.verse_text: By the blessing of the upright a city is built up, but by the mouth of the wicked it is torn down.
data.commentary_text: By the blessing--implying active benevolence.

data.verse_text: Whoever shows contempt for his neighbor lacks judgment, but a man of understanding remains silent.
data.commentary_text:
despiseth--or, "reviles," a course contrasted with the prudent silence of the wise. 

holdeth his peace--as if neither hearing nor telling.

data.verse_text: A gossip reveals a secret, but a trustworthy person keeps a confidence.
data.commentary_text: tale-bearer--(Compare Margin), one trading as a peddler in scandal, whose propensity to talk leads him to betray confidence.

data.verse_text: For lack of guidance, a nation falls, but with many counselors comes deliverance.
data.commentary_text:
counsel--the art of governing (Pro 1:5). 

counsellors--literally, "one giving counsel"; the participle used as a collective.

data.verse_text: He who puts up security for a stranger will surely suffer, but the one who hates indebtedness is secure.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 6:1). 

suretiship--(Compare Margin), the actors put for the action, which may be lawfully hated.

data.verse_text: A gracious woman attains honor, but ruthless men gain only wealth.
data.commentary_text: retaineth--or literally, "lay hold of as a support." Honor is to a feeble woman thus as valuable as riches to men.

data.verse_text: A kind man benefits himself, but a cruel man brings trouble on himself.
data.commentary_text:
merciful--kind to others; opposed to cruel. Such benefit themselves by doing good to others (compare Pro 24:5), while the cruel injure themselves as well as others. 

flesh--that is, his body, by penuriousness (Col 2:23).

data.verse_text: The wicked man earns an empty wage, but he who sows righteousness reaps a true reward.
data.commentary_text:
a deceitful work--or, "wages," which fail to satisfy, or flee away (Pro 10:2; Pro 23:5). 

sure reward--or, "gain," as from trading (Hos 10:12; Gal 6:8-9).

data.verse_text: Genuine righteousness leads to life, but the pursuit of evil brings death.
data.commentary_text: Inference from Pro 11:18 (compare Pro 11:5-6; Pro 10:16).

data.verse_text: The perverse in heart are an abomination to the LORD, but the blameless in their walk are His delight.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 11:5). 

froward--as in Pro 2:15, opposed to the simplicity and purity of the upright. 

in their way--or, "conduct."

data.verse_text: Be assured that the wicked will not go unpunished, but the offspring of the righteous will escape.
data.commentary_text: The combined power of the wicked cannot free them from just punishment, while the unaided children of the righteous find deliverance by reason of their pious relationship (Psa 37:25-26).

data.verse_text: Like a gold ring in a pig’s snout is a beautiful woman who lacks discretion.
data.commentary_text: Jewels were often suspended from the nose (Gen 24:47; Isa 3:21). Thus adorned, a hog disgusts less than a fair and indiscreet woman.

data.verse_text: The desire of the righteous leads only to good, but the hope of the wicked brings wrath.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 10:28). 

wrath--is that of God.

data.verse_text: One gives freely, yet gains even more; another withholds what is right, only to become poor.
data.commentary_text: The scope of the whole is a comment on Pro 11:23. Thus liberality, by God's blessing, secures increase, while penuriousness, instead of expected gain, procures poverty.

data.verse_text: A generous soul will prosper, and he who refreshes others will himself be refreshed.
data.commentary_text:
liberal soul--(Compare Margin). 

made fat--prospers (Pro 28:25; Deu 32:15; Luk 6:38). 

watereth . . . watered--a common figure for blessing.

data.verse_text: The people will curse the hoarder of grain, but blessing will crown the one who sells it.
data.commentary_text:
Another example of the truth of Pro 11:23; the miser loses reputation, though he saves corn. 

selleth it--that is, at a fair price.

data.verse_text: He who searches out good finds favor, but evil will come to him who seeks it.
data.commentary_text:
good [and] mischief--that is, of others. 

procureth . . . seeketh--implying success.

data.verse_text: He who trusts in his riches will fall, but the righteous will thrive like foliage.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 10:15; Psa 49:6; Ti1 6:17). 

righteous . . . branch-- (Psa 1:3; Jer 17:8).

data.verse_text: He who brings trouble on his house will inherit the wind, and the fool will be servant to the wise of heart.
data.commentary_text:
troubleth--as Pro 15:27 explains, by greediness for gain (compare Pro 11:17). 

inherit . . . wind--Even successful, his gains are of no real value. So the fool, thus acting, either comes to poverty, or heaps up for others.

data.verse_text: The fruit of the righteous is a tree of life, and he who wins souls is wise.
data.commentary_text:
a tree of life--Blessings to others proceed from the works of the righteous (Pro 3:18). 

winneth souls--(Compare Margin) to do them good as opposed to Pro 6:25; Eze 13:18 (compare Luk 5:10).

data.verse_text: If the righteous receive their due on earth, how much more the ungodly and the sinner!
data.commentary_text:
Behold--Thus calling attention to the illustrations (compare Pro 11:23), the sentiment of which is confirmed even in time, not excluding future rewards and punishments.




Next: Proverbs Chapter 12

data.verse_text: Whoever loves discipline loves knowledge, but he who hates correction is stupid. The good man obtains favor from the LORD, but the LORD condemns a man who devises evil.
data.commentary_text:
(Pro. 12:1-28)

loveth knowledge--as the fruit of instruction or training (Pro 1:2). 

hateth reproof-- (Pro 10:17). 

brutish--stupid, regardless of his own welfare (Psa 49:10; Psa 73:22).

data.verse_text: A man cannot be established through wickedness, but the righteous cannot be uprooted.
data.commentary_text:
Wickedness cannot give permanent prosperity. 

root . . . not be moved--firm as a flourishing tree-- (Psa 1:3; Psa 15:5; Jer 17:8).

data.verse_text: A wife of noble character is her husband’s crown, but she who causes shame is like decay in his bones.
data.commentary_text:
A virtuous woman--in the wide sense of well-disposed to all moral duties (Pro 31:10). 

maketh ashamed--that is, by misconduct. 

rottenness--an incurable evil.

data.verse_text: The plans of the righteous are just, but the counsel of the wicked leads to deceit.
data.commentary_text:
thoughts--or, "purposes." 

are right--literally, "are judgment," that is, true decisions. 

counsels--(Compare Pro 11:14). 

deceit--contrary to truth and honesty.

data.verse_text: The words of the wicked lie in wait for blood, but the speech of the upright rescues them.
data.commentary_text:
The words--or, "expressed designs" of the wicked are for evil purposes. 

the mouth--or, "words" of the righteous delivering instead of ensnaring men.

data.verse_text: The wicked are overthrown and perish, but the house of the righteous will stand.
data.commentary_text: Such conduct brings a proper return, by the destruction of the wicked and well-being of the righteous and his family.

data.verse_text: A man is praised according to his wisdom, but a twisted mind is despised.
data.commentary_text:
despised--as opposed to commended (Pro 11:12). 

perverse heart--or, "wicked principles," as opposed to one of wisdom.

data.verse_text: Better to be lightly esteemed yet have a servant, than to be self-important but lack food.
data.commentary_text:
despised--held in little repute, obscure (Sa1 18:23; Isa 3:5). 

hath a servant--implying some means of honest living. 

honoureth himself--is self-conceited.

data.verse_text: A righteous man regards the life of his animal, but the tender mercies of the wicked are only cruelty.
data.commentary_text:
regardeth--literally, "knoweth" (Psa 1:6). 

mercies . . . cruel--as acts of compassion ungraciously rendered to the needy. The righteous more regards a beast than the wicked a man.

data.verse_text: The one who works his land will have plenty of food, but whoever chases fantasies lacks judgment.
data.commentary_text: The idler's fate is the result of indolence and want of principle (Pro 6:32; Pro 7:7).

data.verse_text: The wicked desire the plunder of evil men, but the root of the righteous flourishes.
data.commentary_text:
the wicked . . . evil--They love the crafty arts of deception. 

the root . . . fruit--their own resources supply them; or, it may be rendered: "He (God) giveth, or, sets (Eze 17:22) the root of the righteous," and hence it is firm: or, the verb is impersonal; "As to the root . . . it is firm" (Pro 17:19).

data.verse_text: An evil man is trapped by his rebellious speech, but a righteous man escapes from trouble. By fruitful speech a man is filled with good things, and the work of his hands returns to him.
data.commentary_text: The wicked is snared, &c.--The sentiment expanded. While the wicked, such as liars, flatterers, &c., fall by their own words, the righteous are unhurt. Their good conduct makes friends, and God rewards them.

data.verse_text: The way of a fool is right in his own eyes, but a wise man listens to counsel.
data.commentary_text: The way . . . eyes--The fool is self-conceited (compare Pro 12:1; Pro 1:32; Pro 10:17; Jam 3:17).

data.verse_text: A fool’s anger is known at once, but a prudent man overlooks an insult. He who speaks the truth declares what is right, but a false witness speaks deceit.
data.commentary_text: prudent . . . shame--He is slow to denounce his insulters (Jam 1:19).

data.verse_text: Speaking rashly is like a piercing sword, but the tongue of the wise brings healing.
data.commentary_text:
speaketh--literally, "speaketh hastily," or indiscreetly (Psa 106:33), as an angry man retorts harsh and provoking invectives. 

tongue . . . health--by soothing and gentle language.
