data.verse_text: Truthful lips endure forever, but a lying tongue lasts only a moment.
data.commentary_text: Words of truth are consistent, and stand all tests, while lies are soon discovered and exposed.

data.verse_text: Deceit is in the hearts of those who devise evil, but the counselors of peace have joy.
data.commentary_text: that imagine--or, "plan" (Pro 3:29). They design a deceitful course, to which, with all its evils and dangers to others and themselves, the happiness of peace-makers is opposed (compare Mat 5:9; Rom 12:18).

data.verse_text: No harm befalls the righteous, but the wicked are filled with trouble.
data.commentary_text:
no evil--(as in Psa 91:10), under God's wise limitations (Rom 8:28). 

mischief--as penal evil.

data.verse_text: Lying lips are detestable to the LORD, but those who deal faithfully are His delight.
data.commentary_text: deal truly--or, "faithfully," that is, according to promises (compare Joh 3:21).

data.verse_text: A shrewd man keeps his knowledge to himself, but a foolish heart proclaims its folly.
data.commentary_text:
concealeth--by his modesty (Pro 10:14; Pro 11:13). 

heart . . . proclaimeth--as his lips speak his thoughts (compare Ecc 10:3).

data.verse_text: The hand of the diligent will rule, but laziness ends in forced labor.
data.commentary_text:
slothful--(Compare Margin), so called because he fails to meet his promises. 

under tribute--not denoting legal taxes, but the obligation of dependence.

data.verse_text: Anxiety weighs down the heart of a man, but a good word cheers it up.
data.commentary_text: a good word--one of comfort.

data.verse_text: A righteous man is cautious in friendship, but the ways of the wicked lead them astray.
data.commentary_text: more excellent--(Compare Margin); or, "more successful," while the wicked fail; or, we may read it: "The righteous guides his friend, but," &c., that is, The ability of the righteous to aid others is contrasted with the ruin to which the way of the wicked leads themselves.

data.verse_text: A lazy man does not roast his game, but a diligent man prizes his possession.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 12:24). 

took in hunting--or, "his venison." He does not improve his advantages. 

the substance . . . precious--or, "the wealth of a man of honor is being diligent," or "diligence." 

precious--literally, "honor" (Ecc 10:1).

data.verse_text: There is life in the path of righteousness, but another path leads to death.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 8:8, Pro 8:20, &c.). A sentiment often stated; here first affirmatively, then negatively.




Next: Proverbs Chapter 13

data.verse_text: A wise son heeds his father’s discipline, but a mocker does not listen to rebuke.
From the fruit of his lips a man enjoys good things, but the desire of the faithless is violence.
data.commentary_text:
shall eat--that is, obtain (Pro 12:14). 

transgressors--as in Pro 2:22. 

violence--or, "mischief" to themselves.

data.verse_text: He who guards his mouth protects his life, but the one who opens his lips invites his own ruin.
data.commentary_text:
He . . . mouth . . . life--because evil speeches may provoke violence from others. 

he that openeth wide his lips shall have destruction--On last clause, compare Pro 10:14.

data.verse_text: The slacker craves yet has nothing, but the soul of the diligent is fully satisfied.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 12:11, Pro 12:27).

data.verse_text: The righteous hate falsehood, but the wicked bring shame and disgrace.
data.commentary_text: loathsome . . . shame--better, causeth shame and reproach (compare Pro 19:26), by slander, &c., which the righteous hates.

data.verse_text: Righteousness guards the man of integrity, but wickedness undermines the sinner.
data.commentary_text: A sentiment of frequent recurrence, that piety benefits and sin injures.

data.verse_text: One pretends to be rich, but has nothing; another pretends to be poor, yet has great wealth.
data.commentary_text: In opposite ways men act hypocritically for gain of honor or wealth.

data.verse_text: Riches may ransom a man’s life, but a poor man hears no threat.
data.commentary_text: Riches save some from punishment, while others suffer because they will not heed the rebuke of sloth, which makes and keeps them poor.

data.verse_text: The light of the righteous shines brightly, but the lamp of the wicked is extinguished.
data.commentary_text:
light . . . lamp--prosperity; the first, the greater, and it 

rejoiceth--burns brightly, or continues, while the other, at best small, soon fails.

data.verse_text: Arrogance leads only to strife, but wisdom is with the well-advised.
data.commentary_text: The obstinacy which attends self-conceit, produces contention, which the well-advised, thus evincing modesty, avoid.

data.verse_text: Dishonest wealth will dwindle, but what is earned through hard work will be multiplied.
data.commentary_text:
by vanity--or, "nothingness," that is, which is vain or useless to the public (as card playing or similar vices). 

gathereth . . . labour--(Compare Margin), little by little, laboriously.

data.verse_text: Hope deferred makes the heart sick, but desire fulfilled is a tree of life.
data.commentary_text:
desire cometh--is realized. 

a tree of life--or, "cause of happiness."

data.verse_text: He who despises instruction will pay the penalty, but the one who respects a command will be rewarded.
data.commentary_text: the word--that is, of advice, or, instruction (compare Pro 10:27; Pro 11:31).

data.verse_text: The teaching of the wise is a fountain of life, turning one from the snares of death.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 10:11). 

fountain--or, "source of life." 

to depart--(compare Pro 1:2-4), or, "for departing," &c., and so gives life.

data.verse_text: Good understanding wins favor, but the way of the faithless is difficult.
data.commentary_text:
Right perception and action secure good will, while evil ways are difficult as a stony road. The wicked left of God find punishment of sin in sinning. 

hard--or, "harsh" (compare Hebrew: Deu 21:4; Jer 5:15).

data.verse_text: Every prudent man acts with knowledge, but a fool displays his folly.
data.commentary_text:
dealeth--acts with foresight. 

a fool . . . folly--for want of caution.

data.verse_text: A wicked messenger falls into trouble, but a faithful envoy brings healing.
data.commentary_text:
A wicked--or, "unfaithful" 

messenger falleth into mischief--or, "by mischief," or "evil," and so his errand fails. Contrasted is the character of the faithful, whose faithfulness benefits others.

data.verse_text: Poverty and shame come to him who ignores discipline, but whoever heeds correction is honored.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 10:17; Pro 12:1).

data.verse_text: Desire fulfilled is sweet to the soul, but turning from evil is detestable to fools.
data.commentary_text: Self-denial, which fools will not endure, is essential to success.

data.verse_text: He who walks with the wise will become wise, but the companion of fools will be destroyed.
data.commentary_text: The benefits of good and evil of bad society are contrasted.

data.verse_text: Disaster pursues sinners, but prosperity is the reward of the righteous.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 11:31). 

good . . . repaid--or, "He (God) will repay good."

data.verse_text: A good man leaves an inheritance to his children’s children, but the sinner’s wealth is passed to the righteous.
data.commentary_text: wealth . . . just--While good men's estates remain in their families, God so orders that the gains of sinners enure to the just (compare Pro 28:8; Psa 37:18, Psa 37:22, Psa 37:26, &c.).

data.verse_text: Abundant food is in the fallow ground of the poor, but without justice it is swept away.
data.commentary_text: The laboring poor prosper more than those who injudiciously or wickedly strive, by fraud and violence, to supersede the necessity of lawful labor.

data.verse_text: He who spares the rod hates his son, but he who loves him disciplines him diligently.
data.commentary_text:
spareth--or, "withholds." 

rod--of correction. 

hateth--or, acts as if he hated him (compare Pro 3:12; Pro 8:36). 

chasteneth . . . betimes--or, "diligently seeks for him all useful discipline."

data.verse_text: A righteous man eats to his heart’s content, but the stomach of the wicked is empty.
data.commentary_text:
The comparative temporal prosperity of the righteous and wicked, rather than contentment and discontent, is noted.




Next: Proverbs Chapter 14

data.verse_text: Every wise woman builds her house, but a foolish one tears it down with her own hands.
data.commentary_text:
(Pro. 14:1-35)

Every wise, &c.--literally, "The wisdoms" (compare Pro 9:1) "of women," plural, a distributive form of speech. 

buildeth . . . house--increases wealth, which the foolish, by mismanagement, lessen.

data.verse_text: He who walks in uprightness fears the LORD, but the one who is devious in his ways despises Him.
data.commentary_text: uprightness--is the fruit of fearing God, as falsehood and ill-nature (Pro 2:15; Pro 3:32) of despising Him and His law.

data.verse_text: The proud speech of a fool brings a rod to his back, but the lips of the wise protect them.
data.commentary_text: rod of pride--that is, the punishment of pride, which they evince by their words. The words of the wise procure good to them.

data.verse_text: Where there are no oxen, the manger is empty, but an abundant harvest comes through the strength of the ox.
data.commentary_text: crib is clean--empty; so "cleanness of teeth" denotes want of food (compare Amo 4:6). Men get the proper fruit of their doings (Gal 6:7).

data.verse_text: An honest witness does not deceive, but a dishonest witness pours forth lies.
data.commentary_text:
A faithful witness, &c.--one tested to be such. 

utter lies--or, "breathe out lies"--that is, habitually lies (Pro 6:19; compare Act 9:1). Or the sense is, that habitual truthfulness, or lying, will be evinced in witness-bearing.

data.verse_text: A mocker seeks wisdom and finds none, but knowledge comes easily to the discerning.
data.commentary_text: An humble, teachable spirit succeeds in seeking (Pro 8:9; Joh 7:17; Jam 1:5-6).

data.verse_text: Stay away from a foolish man; you will gain no knowledge from his speech.
data.commentary_text: Avoid the society of those who cannot teach you.

data.verse_text: The wisdom of the prudent is to discern his way, but the folly of fools deceives them.
data.commentary_text: Appearances deceive the thoughtless, but the prudent discriminate.

data.verse_text: Fools mock the making of amends, but goodwill is found among the upright.
data.commentary_text:
Fools make a mock at sin--or, "Sin deludes fools." 

righteous . . . favour--that is, of God, instead of the punishment of sin.

data.verse_text: The heart knows its own bitterness, and no stranger shares in its joy.
data.commentary_text: Each one best knows his own sorrows or joys.

data.verse_text: The house of the wicked will be destroyed, but the tent of the upright will flourish.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 12:7). The contrast of the whole is enhanced by that of house and tabernacle, a permanent and a temporary dwelling.

data.verse_text: There is a way that seems right to a man, but its end is the way of death.
data.commentary_text:
end thereof--or, "reward," what results (compare Pro 5:4). 

ways of death--leading to it.

data.verse_text: Even in laughter the heart may ache, and joy may end in sorrow.
data.commentary_text: The preceding sentiment illustrated by the disappointments of a wicked or untimely joy.

data.verse_text: The backslider in heart receives the fill of his own ways, but a good man is rewarded for his ways.
data.commentary_text:
filled . . . ways--receive retribution (Pro 1:31). 

a good man . . . himself--literally, "is away from such," will not associate with him.

data.verse_text: The simple man believes every word, but the prudent man watches his steps.
data.commentary_text: The simple . . . word--He is credulous, not from love, but heedlessness (Pro 13:16).

data.verse_text: A wise man fears and turns from evil, but a fool is careless and reckless.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 3:7; Pro 28:14). 

rageth--acts proudly and conceitedly.

data.verse_text: A quick-tempered man acts foolishly, and a devious man is hated.
data.commentary_text:
He . . . angry--literally, "short of anger" (compare Pro 14:29, opposite idea). 

man . . . hated--that is, the deliberate evildoer is more hated than the rash.

data.verse_text: The simple inherit folly, but the prudent are crowned with knowledge.
data.commentary_text:
inherit--as a portion (compare Pro 3:35). 

are crowned--literally, "are surrounded with it," abound in it.

data.verse_text: The evil bow before the good, and the wicked at the gates of the righteous.
data.commentary_text: Describes the humbling of the wicked by the punishment their sins incur.

data.verse_text: The poor man is hated even by his neighbor, but many are those who love the rich.
data.commentary_text: This sad but true picture of human nature is not given approvingly, but only as a fact.

data.verse_text: He who despises his neighbor sins, but blessed is he who shows kindness to the poor.
data.commentary_text: For such contempt of the poor is contrasted as sinful with the virtuous compassion of the good.

data.verse_text: Do not those who contrive evil go astray? But those who plan goodness find loving devotion and faithfulness.
data.commentary_text:
As usual, the interrogative negative strengthens the affirmative. 

mercy and truth--that is, God's (Psa 57:3; Psa 61:7).

data.verse_text: There is profit in all labor, but mere talk leads only to poverty.
data.commentary_text:
labour--painful diligence. 

talk . . . penury--idle and vain promises and plans.

data.verse_text: The crown of the wise is their wealth, but the effort of fools is folly.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 3:16). 

foolishness . . . folly--Folly remains, or produces folly; it has no benefit.

data.verse_text: A truthful witness saves lives, but one who utters lies is deceitful.
data.commentary_text:
Life often depends on truth-telling. 

a deceitful . . . lies--He that breathes out lies is deceit, not to be trusted (Pro 14:5).

data.verse_text: He who fears the LORD is secure in confidence, and his children shall have a place of refuge.
data.commentary_text: The blessings of piety descend to children (Pro 13:22; Pro 20:7; Exo 20:6).

data.verse_text: The fear of the LORD is a fountain of life, turning a man from the snares of death.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 13:14). 

fear of the Lord--or, "law of the wise," is wisdom (Psa 111:10).

data.verse_text: A large population is a king’s splendor, but a lack of subjects is a prince’s ruin.
data.commentary_text: The teaching of a true political economy.

data.verse_text: A patient man has great understanding, but a quick-tempered man promotes folly.
data.commentary_text:
slow . . . understanding--(Compare Pro 14:17). 

hasty--(Compare Pro 14:17). 

exalteth folly--makes it conspicuous, as if delighting to honor it.

data.verse_text: A tranquil heart is life to the body, but envy rots the bones.
data.commentary_text: A sound heart--both literally and figuratively, a source of health; in the latter sense, opposed to the known effect of evil passions on health.

data.verse_text: Whoever oppresses the poor taunts their Maker, but whoever is kind to the needy honors Him.
data.commentary_text: reproacheth his Maker--who is the God of such, as well as of the rich (Pro 22:2; Job 31:15; and specially Sa1 2:8; Psa 113:7).

data.verse_text: The wicked man is thrown down by his own sin, but the righteous man has a refuge even in death.
data.commentary_text:
driven--thrust out violently (compare Psa 35:5-6). 

hath hope--trusteth (Pro 10:2; Pro 11:4; Psa 2:12), implying assurance of help.

data.verse_text: Wisdom rests in the heart of the discerning; even among fools she is known.
data.commentary_text: resteth--preserved in quietness for use, while fools blazon their folly (Pro 12:23; Pro 13:16).

data.verse_text: Righteousness exalts a nation, but sin is a disgrace to any people.
data.commentary_text:
Righteousness--just principles and actions. 

exalteth--raises to honor. 

is a reproach--brings on them the ill-will of others (compare Pro 13:6).

data.verse_text: A king delights in a wise servant, but his anger falls on the shameful.
data.commentary_text:
wise--discreet or prudent. 

causeth shame-- (Pro 10:5; Pro 12:4) acts basely.




Next: Proverbs Chapter 15

data.verse_text: A gentle answer turns away wrath, but a harsh word stirs up anger.
data.commentary_text:
(Pro. 15:1-33)

soft--tender or gentle. 

turneth . . . wrath--from any one. 

stir up--as a smouldering fire is excited.

data.verse_text: The tongue of the wise commends knowledge, but the mouth of the fool spouts folly.
data.commentary_text:
useth . . . aright--commends knowledge by its proper use. 

poureth out--utters abundantly (Pro 12:23), and so disgusts others.

data.verse_text: The eyes of the LORD are in every place, observing the evil and the good.
data.commentary_text: beholding--watching (compare Pro 5:21; Psa 66:7).

data.verse_text: A soothing tongue is a tree of life, but a perverse tongue crushes the spirit.
data.commentary_text:
A wholesome tongue--(Compare Margin), pacifying and soothing language. 

tree of life-- (Pro 3:18; Pro 11:30). 

perverseness therein--cross, ill-natured language. 

breach . . . spirit--(compare Isa 65:14, Hebrew), grieves, instead of appeasing.

data.verse_text: A fool rejects his father’s discipline, but whoever heeds correction is prudent.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 4:1; Pro 10:17; Pro. 13:1-18). 

is prudent--acts discreetly.

data.verse_text: The house of the righteous has great treasure, but the income of the wicked is trouble.
data.commentary_text:
treasure--implying utility. 

trouble--vexation and affliction.

data.verse_text: The lips of the wise spread knowledge, but not so the hearts of fools.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 10:20-21). 

heart . . . not so--not right, or vain.

data.verse_text: The sacrifice of the wicked is detestable to the LORD, but the prayer of the upright is His delight. The LORD detests the way of the wicked, but He loves those who pursue righteousness.
data.commentary_text:
The sacrifice [and] prayer--are acts of worship. 

way . . . followeth . . . righteousness--denote conduct. God's regard for the worship and deeds of the righteous and wicked respectively, so stated in Psa 50:17; Isa 1:11.

data.verse_text: Discipline is harsh for him who leaves the path; he who hates correction will die.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 10:17). 

the way--that in which God would have him to go (Pro 2:13; Psa 119:1).

data.verse_text: Sheol and Abaddon lie open before the LORD— how much more the hearts of men!
data.commentary_text:
Hell-- (Psa 16:10). 

destruction--or, "Abaddon," the place of the destroyer. All the unseen world is open to God, much more men's hearts.

data.verse_text: A mocker does not love to be reproved, nor will he consult the wise.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 9:8). 

go unto the wise--to be instructed.

data.verse_text: A joyful heart makes a cheerful countenance, but sorrow of the heart crushes the spirit.
data.commentary_text:
maketh . . . countenance--or, "benefits the countenance." 

spirit is broken--and so the countenance is sad.

data.verse_text: A discerning heart seeks knowledge, but the mouth of a fool feeds on folly.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 10:21-22). The wise grow wiser, the fools more foolish (Pro 9:9).

data.verse_text: All the days of the oppressed are bad, but a cheerful heart has a continual feast.
data.commentary_text:
The state of the heart governs the outward condition. 

evil--sad, contrasted with the cheerfulness of a feast.

data.verse_text: Better a little with the fear of the LORD than great treasure with turmoil.
data.commentary_text: trouble--agitation, implying the anxieties and perplexities attending wealth held by worldlings (Pro 16:18; Ti1 6:6).

data.verse_text: Better a dish of vegetables where there is love than a fattened ox with hatred.
data.commentary_text:
dinner--or, "allowance" (Kg2 25:30) -- 

of herbs--and that the plainest. 

and hatred--(compare Pro 10:12, Pro 10:18).

data.verse_text: A hot-tempered man stirs up strife, but he who is slow to anger calms dispute.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 14:29; Pro 16:32).

data.verse_text: The way of the slacker is like a hedge of thorns, but the path of the upright is a highway.
data.commentary_text:
The difficulties of the slothful result from want of energy; the righteous find a 

plain [and open] way--literally, "a highway," by diligence (Sa1 10:7; Psa 1:3).

data.verse_text: A wise son brings joy to his father, but a foolish man despises his mother.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 10:1).

data.verse_text: Folly is joy to one who lacks judgment, but a man of understanding walks a straight path.
data.commentary_text: walketh uprightly--and so finds his joy (Pro 3:6; Pro 10:23).

data.verse_text: Plans fail for lack of counsel, but with many advisers they succeed.
data.commentary_text: Without counsel--or, "deliberation," implying a wise deference to the opinions of the wise and good, contrasted with rashness.

data.verse_text: A man takes joy in a fitting reply— and how good is a timely word!
data.commentary_text: Good advice blesses the giver and receiver.

data.verse_text: The path of life leads upward for the wise, that he may avoid going down to Sheol.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Col 3:2). Holy purposes prevent sinning, and so its evils.

data.verse_text: The LORD tears down the house of the proud, but He protects the boundaries of the widow.
data.commentary_text:
The most desolate who have God's aid have more permanent good than the self-reliant sinner (Pro 2:22; Pro 12:7). 

border--or, "boundary for possessions" (Psa 78:54).

data.verse_text: The LORD detests the thoughts of the wicked, but the words of the pure are pleasant to Him.
data.commentary_text: are pleasant words--that is, pleasing to God (Pro 8:8-9).

data.verse_text: He who is greedy for unjust gain brings trouble on his household, but he who hates bribes will live.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 11:17). Avarice brings trouble to him and his. 

hateth gifts--or, "bribes" (Exo 23:8; Psa 15:5), and is not avaricious.

data.verse_text: The heart of the righteous ponders how to answer, but the mouth of the wicked blurts out evil.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 15:14; Pro 10:11). Caution is the fruit of wisdom; rashness of folly.

data.verse_text: The LORD is far from the wicked, but He hears the prayer of the righteous.
data.commentary_text: far . . . wicked--in His love and favor (Psa 22:11; Psa 119:155).

data.verse_text: The light of the eyes cheers the heart, and good news nourishes the bones.
data.commentary_text:
light of the eyes-- (Pro 13:9). What gives light rejoiceth the heart, by relieving from anxiety as to our course; so 

good report--or, "doctrine" (Isa 28:9; Isa 53:1), 

maketh . . . fat--or, "gives prosperity" (Pro 3:13-17; Pro 9:11). The last clause is illustrated by the first.

data.verse_text: He who listens to life-giving reproof will dwell among the wise.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 10:17). 

reproof of life--which leads to life. 

abideth . . . wise--is numbered among them.

data.verse_text: He who ignores discipline despises himself, but whoever heeds correction gains understanding.
data.commentary_text:
refuseth--or, "neglects," "passes by" (Pro 1:25; Pro 4:15). 

despiseth . . . soul--so acts as if esteeming its interests of no value.

data.verse_text: The fear of the LORD is the instruction of wisdom, and humility comes before honor.
data.commentary_text:
The fear . . . wisdom--Wisdom instructs in true piety. 

before . . . humility--(compare Luk 24:26; Pe1 1:11); opposite (compare Pro 16:18).




Next: Proverbs Chapter 16

data.verse_text: The plans of the heart belong to man, but the reply of the tongue is from the LORD.
data.commentary_text:
(Pro. 16:1-33)

preparations--schemes. 

in man--or literally, "to man," belonging, or pertaining to him. 

the answer . . . Lord--The efficient ordering is from God: "Man proposes; God disposes."

data.verse_text: All a man’s ways are pure in his own eyes, but his motives are weighed out by the LORD.
data.commentary_text:
clean--or, "faultless." 

weigheth--or, "tries," "judges," implying that they are faulty (Pro 21:2; Pro 24:12).

data.verse_text: Commit your works to the LORD and your plans will be achieved.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Margin). Rely on God for success to your lawful purposes.

data.verse_text: The LORD has made everything for His purpose— even the wicked for the day of disaster.
data.commentary_text: for himself--"for its answer," or "purpose," that is, according to God's plan; the wicked are for the day of evil (Psa 49:5; Jer 17:18); sinning and suffering answer to each other, are indissolubly united.

data.verse_text: Everyone who is proud in heart is detestable to the LORD; be assured that he will not go unpunished.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 3:32).

data.verse_text: By loving devotion and faithfulness iniquity is atoned for, and by the fear of the LORD one turns aside from evil.
data.commentary_text:
By mercy and truth--that is, God's (Psa 85:10); He effects the atonement, or covering of sin; and the principles of true piety incline men to depart from evil; or, "mercy" and "truth" may be man's, indicative of the gracious tempers which work instrumentally in procuring pardon. 

purged--expiated (as in Lev 16:33; Isa 27:9, Hebrew).

data.verse_text: When a man’s ways please the LORD, He makes even the man’s enemies live at peace with him.
data.commentary_text: Persecutions, of course, excepted.

data.verse_text: Better a little with righteousness than great gain with injustice.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 15:6, Pro 15:16-17).

data.verse_text: A man’s heart plans his course, but the LORD determines his steps.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 16:3). 

directeth--establisheth.

data.verse_text: A divine verdict is on the lips of a king; his mouth must not betray justice.
data.commentary_text: The last clause depends on the first, expressing the importance of equity in decisions, so authoritative.

data.verse_text: Honest scales and balances are from the LORD; all the weights in the bag are His concern.
data.commentary_text: are the Lord's . . . his work--that is, what He has ordered, and hence should be observed by men.

data.verse_text: Wicked behavior is detestable to kings, for a throne is established through righteousness.
data.commentary_text: Rulers are rightly expected, by their position, to hate evil; for their power is sustained by righteousness.

data.verse_text: Righteous lips are a king’s delight, and he who speaks honestly is beloved.
data.commentary_text: A specification of the general sentiment of Pro 16:12.

data.verse_text: The wrath of a king is a messenger of death, but a wise man will pacify it.
data.commentary_text: This wrath, so terrible and certain, like messengers of death (Kg1 2:25), can be appeased by the wise.

data.verse_text: When a king’s face brightens, there is life; his favor is like a rain cloud in spring.
data.commentary_text:
light of . . . countenance--favor (Psa 4:6). 

life--preserves it, or gives blessings which make it valuable. 

the latter rain--fell just before harvest and matured the crop; hence specially valuable (Deu 11:14).

data.verse_text: How much better to acquire wisdom than gold! To gain understanding is more desirable than silver.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 3:16; Pro 4:5).

data.verse_text: The highway of the upright leads away from evil; he who guards his way protects his life.
data.commentary_text:
The highway--A common, plain road represents the habitual course of the righteous in departing from evil. 

keepeth--observes.

data.verse_text: Pride goes before destruction, and a haughty spirit before a fall.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 15:33). Haughtiness and pride imply self-confidence which produces carelessness, and hence 

a fall--literally, "sliding."

data.verse_text: It is better to be lowly in spirit among the humble than to divide the spoil with the proud.
data.commentary_text: divide the spoil--that is, conquer. Avoid the society of the proud (Jam 4:6).

data.verse_text: Whoever heeds instruction will find success, and blessed is he who trusts in the LORD.
data.commentary_text:
handleth a matter--wisely considers "the word," that is, of God (compare Pro 13:13). 

trusteth--(Compare Psa 2:12; Psa 118:8-9).

data.verse_text: The wise in heart are called discerning, and pleasant speech promotes instruction.
data.commentary_text:
wise in heart--who rightly consider duty. 

sweetness of the lips--eloquent discourse, persuades and instructs others.

data.verse_text: Understanding is a fountain of life to its possessor, but the discipline of fools is folly.
data.commentary_text: Understanding--or, "discretion," is a constant source of blessing (Pro 13:14), benefiting others; but fools' best efforts are folly.

data.verse_text: The heart of the wise man instructs his mouth and adds persuasiveness to his lips.
data.commentary_text: The heart is the source of wisdom flowing from the mouth.

data.verse_text: Pleasant words are a honeycomb, sweet to the soul and healing to the bones.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 15:26). Gentle, kind words, by soothing the mind, give the body health.

data.verse_text: There is a way that seems right to a man, but its end is the way of death.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 14:2).

data.verse_text: A worker’s appetite works for him because his hunger drives him onward.
data.commentary_text: Diligence is a duty due to one's self, for his wants require labor.

data.verse_text: A worthless man digs up evil, and his speech is like a scorching fire.
data.commentary_text:
ungodly man--(Compare Pro 6:12). 

diggeth up evil--labors for it. 

in his lips . . . fire--His words are calumniating (Jam 3:6).

data.verse_text: A perverse man spreads dissension, and a gossip divides close friends.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 6:14; Pro 10:31). 

whisperer--prater, talebearer (Pro 18:8; Pro 26:20).

data.verse_text: A violent man entices his neighbor and leads him down a path that is not good.
data.commentary_text:
violent man--or, "man of mischief" (Pro 3:31). 

enticeth-- (Pro 1:10).

data.verse_text: He who winks his eye devises perversity; he who purses his lips is bent on evil.
data.commentary_text:
He shutteth his eyes--denoting deep thought (Psa 64:6). 

moving his lips--or, "biting his lips"--a determined purpose (Pro 6:13).

data.verse_text: Gray hair is a crown of glory; it is attained along the path of righteousness.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 20:29). 

if--or, which may be supplied properly, or without it the sense is as in Pro 3:16; Pro 4:10, that piety is blessed with long life.

data.verse_text: He who is slow to anger is better than a warrior, and he who controls his temper is greater than one who captures a city.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 14:29). 

taketh a city--that is, by fighting.

data.verse_text: The lot is cast into the lap, but its every decision is from the LORD.
data.commentary_text:
Seemingly the most fortuitous events are ordered by God.




Next: Proverbs Chapter 17

data.verse_text: Better a dry morsel in quietness than a house full of feasting with strife.
data.commentary_text:
(Pro. 17:1-28)

sacrifices--or, "feasts" made with part of them (compare Pro 7:14; Lev 2:3; Lev 7:31). 

with--literally, "of." 

strife--its product, or attendant.

data.verse_text: A wise servant will rule over a disgraceful son and share his inheritance as one of the brothers.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 14:35). 

causeth shame-- (Pro 10:5). 

shall . . . inheritance--that is, share a brother's part (compare Num 27:4, Num 27:7).

data.verse_text: A crucible for silver and a furnace for gold, but the LORD is the tester of hearts.
data.commentary_text: God only knows, as He tries (Psa 12:6; Psa 66:10) the heart.

data.verse_text: A wicked man listens to evil lips; a liar gives ear to a destructive tongue.
data.commentary_text: Wicked doers and speakers alike delight in calumny.

data.verse_text: He who mocks the poor insults their Maker; whoever gloats over calamity will not go unpunished.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 14:31). 

glad at calamities--rejoicing in others' evil. Such are rightly punished by God, who knows their hearts.

data.verse_text: Grandchildren are the crown of the aged, and the glory of a son is his father.
data.commentary_text: Prolonged posterity is a blessing, its cutting off a curse (Pro 13:22; Psa 109:13-15), hence children may glory in virtuous ancestry.

data.verse_text: Eloquent words are unfit for a fool; how much worse are lying lips to a ruler!
data.commentary_text: Excellent speech--(Compare Margin). Such language as ill suits a fool, as lying (ought to suit) a prince (Pro 16:12-13).

data.verse_text: A bribe is a charm to its giver; wherever he turns, he succeeds.
data.commentary_text: One so corrupt as to take a bribe evinces his high estimate of it by subjection to its influence (Pro 18:16; Pro 19:6).

data.verse_text: Whoever conceals an offense promotes love, but he who brings it up separates friends.
data.commentary_text: seeketh love--(Compare Margin). The contrast is between the peace-maker and tale-bearer.

data.verse_text: A rebuke cuts into a man of discernment deeper than a hundred lashes cut into a fool.
data.commentary_text: Reproof more affects the wise than severe scourging, fools.

data.verse_text: An evil man seeks only rebellion; a cruel messenger will be sent against him.
data.commentary_text:
Such meet just retribution (Kg1 2:25). 

a cruel messenger--one to inflict it.

data.verse_text: It is better to meet a bear robbed of her cubs than a fool in his folly.
data.commentary_text: They are less rational in anger than wild beasts.

data.verse_text: If anyone returns evil for good, evil will never leave his house.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Psa 7:4; Psa 35:12). 

evil--injury to another (Pro 13:21).

data.verse_text: To start a quarrel is to release a flood; so abandon the dispute before it breaks out.
data.commentary_text:
letteth . . . water--as a breach in a dam. 

before . . . meddled with--before strife has become sharp, or, by an explanation better suiting the figure, before it rolls on, or increases.

data.verse_text: Acquitting the guilty and condemning the righteous— both are detestable to the LORD.
data.commentary_text: abomination . . . Lord--as reversing His method of acting (Pro 3:32; Pro 12:2).

data.verse_text: Why should the fool have money in his hand with no intention of buying wisdom?
data.commentary_text: Though wealth cannot buy wisdom for those who do not love it, yet wisdom procures wealth (Pro 3:16; Pro 14:24).

data.verse_text: A friend loves at all times, and a brother is born for adversity.
data.commentary_text: To the second of these parallel clauses, there is an accession of meaning, that is, that a brother's love is specially seen in adversity.

data.verse_text: A man lacking judgment strikes hands in pledge and puts up security for his neighbor.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 6:1-5; Pro 11:15). 

in the presence, &c.--that is, he either fails to consult his friend, or to follow his advice.

data.verse_text: He who loves transgression loves strife; he who builds his gate high invites destruction.
data.commentary_text:
strife--contention is, and leads to, sin. 

he that exalteth his gate--gratifies a vain love of costly building. 

seeketh--or, "findeth," as if he sought (compare "loveth death," Pro 8:36).

data.verse_text: The one with a perverse heart finds no good, and he whose tongue is deceitful falls into trouble.
data.commentary_text: The second clause advances on the first. The ill-natured fail of good, and the cavilling and fault-finding incur evil.

data.verse_text: A man fathers a fool to his own grief; the father of a fool has no joy.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 23:24). Different words are rendered by "fool," both denoting stupidity and impiety.

data.verse_text: A joyful heart is good medicine, but a broken spirit dries up the bones.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 14:30; Pro 15:13). The effect of the mind on the body is well known. 

medicine--or, "body," which better corresponds with "bone." 

drieth--as if the marrow were exhausted.

data.verse_text: A wicked man takes a covert bribe to subvert the course of justice.
data.commentary_text:
a gift . . . bosom--Money and other valuables were borne in a fold of the garment, called the bosom. 

to pervert--that is, by bribery.

data.verse_text: Wisdom is the focus of the discerning, but the eyes of a fool wander to the ends of the earth.
data.commentary_text: Wisdom . . . him--ever an object of regard, while a fool's affections are unsettled.

data.verse_text: A foolish son brings grief to his father and bitterness to her who bore him.
data.commentary_text: a grief--or cross, vexation (compare Pro 17:21; Pro 10:1).

data.verse_text: It is surely not good to punish the innocent or to flog a noble for his honesty.
data.commentary_text: Also--that is, Equally to be avoided are other sins: punishing good subjects, or resisting good rulers.

data.verse_text: A man of knowledge restrains his words, and a man of understanding maintains a calm spirit. Even a fool is considered wise if he keeps silent, and discerning when he holds his tongue.
data.commentary_text:
Prudence of speech is commended as is an excellent or calm spirit, not excited to vain conversation.




Next: Proverbs Chapter 18

data.verse_text: He who isolates himself pursues selfish desires; he rebels against all sound judgment.
data.commentary_text:
(Pro. 18:1-24)

Through desire . . . seeketh--that is, seeks selfish gratification. 

intermeddleth . . . wisdom--or, "rushes on" (Pro 17:14) against all wisdom, or what is valuable (Pro 2:7).

data.verse_text: A fool does not delight in understanding, but only in airing his opinions.
data.commentary_text: that his heart . . . itself--that is, takes pleasure in revealing his folly (Pro 12:23; Pro 15:2).

data.verse_text: With a wicked man comes contempt as well, and shame is accompanied by disgrace.
data.commentary_text:
So surely are sin and punishment connected (Pro 16:4). 

wicked, for "wickedness," answers to 

ignominy, or the state of such; and 

contempt, the feeling of others to them; and to 

reproach, a manifestation of contempt.

data.verse_text: The words of a man’s mouth are deep waters; the fountain of wisdom is a bubbling brook.
data.commentary_text: Wise speech is like an exhaustless stream of benefit.

data.verse_text: Showing partiality to the wicked is not good, nor is depriving the innocent of justice.
data.commentary_text: accept the person--(Compare Psa 82:2). "It is not good" is to be supplied before "to overthrow."

data.verse_text: A fool’s lips bring him strife, and his mouth invites a beating. A fool’s mouth is his ruin, and his lips are a snare to his soul.
data.commentary_text: The quarrelsome bring trouble on themselves. Their rash language ensnares them (Pro 6:2).

data.verse_text: The words of a gossip are like choice morsels that go down into the inmost being.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 16:28). 

as wounds--not sustained by the Hebrew; better, as "sweet morsels," which men gladly swallow. 

innermost . . . belly--the mind, or heart (compare Pro 20:27-30; Psa 22:14).

data.verse_text: Whoever is slothful in his work is brother to him who destroys.
data.commentary_text:
One by failing to get, the other by wasting wealth, grows poor. 

waster--literally, "master of washing," a prodigal.

data.verse_text: The name of the LORD is a strong tower; the righteous run to it and are safe.
data.commentary_text:
name of the Lord--manifested perfections (Psa 8:1; Psa 20:2), as faithfulness, power, mercy, &c., on which men rely. 

is safe--literally, "set on high, out of danger" (Psa 18:2; Psa 91:4).

data.verse_text: A rich man’s wealth is his fortified city; it is like a high wall in his imagination.
data.commentary_text: contrasts with Pro 18:10 (compare Pro 10:15). Such is a vain trust (compare Psa 73:6).

data.verse_text: Before his downfall a man’s heart is proud, but humility comes before honor.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 15:33; Pro 16:18).

data.verse_text: He who answers a matter before he hears it— this is folly and disgrace to him.
data.commentary_text: Hasty speech evinces self-conceit, and ensures shame (Pro 26:12).

data.verse_text: The spirit of a man can endure his sickness, but who can survive a broken spirit?
data.commentary_text: infirmity--bodily sickness, or outward evil. The spirit, which sustains, being wounded, no support is left, except, as implied, in God.

data.verse_text: The heart of the discerning acquires knowledge, and the ear of the wise seeks it out.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 1:5, Pro 1:15, Pro 1:31).

data.verse_text: A man’s gift opens doors for him, and brings him before great men.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 17:8, Pro 17:23). Disapproval of the fact stated is implied.

data.verse_text: The first to state his case seems right until another comes and cross-examines him.
data.commentary_text:
One-sided statements are not reliable. 

searcheth--thoroughly (Pro 17:9, Pro 17:19).

data.verse_text: Casting the lot ends quarrels and separates strong opponents.
data.commentary_text: The lot--whose disposal is of God (Pro 16:13), may, properly used, be a right mode of settling disputes.

data.verse_text: An offended brother is harder to win than a fortified city, and disputes are like the bars of a castle.
data.commentary_text: No feuds so difficult of adjustment as those of relatives; hence great care should be used to avoid them.

data.verse_text: From the fruit of his mouth a man’s belly is filled; with the harvest from his lips he is satisfied.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 12:14; Pro 13:2). Men's words are the fruit, or, increase of his lips, and when good, benefit them. 

satisfied with--(Compare Pro 1:31; Pro 14:14).

data.verse_text: Life and death are in the power of the tongue, and those who love it will eat its fruit.
data.commentary_text:
Death and life--or, the greatest evil and good. 

that love it--that is, the tongue, or its use for good or evil. 

eat . . . fruit--(Compare Pro 18:19; Jam 1:19).

data.verse_text: He who finds a wife finds a good thing and obtains favor from the LORD.
data.commentary_text: The old versions supply "good" before the "wife," as the last clause and Pro 19:14 imply (compare Pro 31:10).

data.verse_text: The poor man pleads for mercy, but the rich man answers harshly.
data.commentary_text: the rich . . . roughly--He is tolerated because rich, implying that the estimate of men by wealth is wrong.

data.verse_text: A man of many companions may come to ruin, but there is a friend who stays closer than a brother.
data.commentary_text:
A man . . . friendly--better, "A man . . . (is) to, or, may triumph (Psa 108:9), or, shout for joy (Psa 5:11), that is, may congratulate himself." Indeed, there is a Friend who is better than a brother; such is the "Friend of sinners" [Mat 11:19; Luk 7:34], who may have been before the writer's mind.




Next: Proverbs Chapter 19

data.verse_text: Better a poor man who walks with integrity than a fool whose lips are perverse.
data.commentary_text:
(Pro. 19:1-29)

(Compare Pro 28:6). "Rich" for fool here. Integrity is better than riches (Pro 15:16-17; Pro 16:8).

data.verse_text: Even zeal is no good without knowledge, and he who hurries his footsteps misses the mark.
data.commentary_text: The last illustrates the first clause. Rashness, the result of ignorance, brings trouble.

data.verse_text: A man’s own folly subverts his way, yet his heart rages against the LORD.
data.commentary_text: perverteth . . . way--turns him back from right (Pro 13:6; Jam 1:13); and he blames God for his failures.

data.verse_text: Wealth attracts many friends, but a poor man is deserted by his friend.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 14:20). Such facts are often adduced with implied disapprobation.

data.verse_text: A false witness will not go unpunished, and one who utters lies will not escape. Many seek the favor of the prince, and everyone is a friend of the gift giver. All the brothers of a poor man hate him— how much more do his friends avoid him! He may pursue them with pleading, but they are nowhere to be found.
data.commentary_text: Compare Pro 19:9, where perish explains not escape here (compare Psa 88:9-10).

data.verse_text: He who acquires wisdom loves himself; one who safeguards understanding will find success. A false witness will not go unpunished, and one who pours out lies will perish.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Margin; Pro 15:32). 

loveth . . . soul--or, "himself," which he evinces by regarding his best interests. 

keepeth--or, "regards."

data.verse_text: Luxury is unseemly for a fool— how much worse for a slave to rule over princes!
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 17:7). The fool is incapable of properly using pleasure as knowledge, yet for him to have it is less incongruous than the undue elevation of servants. Let each abide in his calling (Co1 7:20).

data.verse_text: A man’s insight gives him patience, and his virtue is to overlook an offense.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 14:29; Pro 16:32). This inculcation of a forgiving spirit shows that true religion is always the same (Mat 5:22-24).

data.verse_text: A king’s rage is like the roar of a lion, but his favor is like dew on the grass.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 16:14-15; Pro 20:2). A motive to submission to lawful authority.

data.verse_text: A foolish son is his father’s ruin, and a quarrelsome wife is like a constant dripping.
data.commentary_text:
calamity--literally, "calamities," varied and many. 

continual dropping--a perpetual annoyance, wearing out patience.

data.verse_text: Houses and wealth are inherited from fathers, but a prudent wife is from the LORD.
data.commentary_text:
A contrast of men's gifts and God's, who, though author of both blessings, confers the latter by His more special providence. 

and--or, "but," implying that the evils of Pro 19:13 are only avoided by His care.

data.verse_text: Laziness brings on deep sleep, and an idle soul will suffer hunger.
data.commentary_text:
a deep sleep--a state of utter indifference. 

idle soul--or, "person" (compare Pro 10:4; Pro 12:24).

data.verse_text: He who keeps a commandment preserves his soul, but he who is careless in his ways will die.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 10:17; Pro 13:13). 

despiseth . . . ways--opposed to keeping or observing, neglects (Pro 16:17) (as unworthy of regard) his moral conduct.

data.verse_text: Kindness to the poor is a loan to the LORD, and He will repay the lender.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 14:21; Psa 37:26). 

hath pity--shown by acts (compare Margin).

data.verse_text: Discipline your son, for in that there is hope; do not be party to his death.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 13:24; Pro 23:13). 

let not . . . spare--literally, "do not lift up thy soul" (Psa 24:4; Psa 25:1), that is, do not desire to his death; a caution to passionate parents against angry chastisement.

data.verse_text: A man of great anger must pay the penalty; if you rescue him, you will have to do so again.
data.commentary_text: Repeated efforts of kindness are lost on ill-natured persons.

data.verse_text: Listen to counsel and accept discipline, that you may be wise the rest of your days.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 13:18-20). 

latter end-- (Pro 5:11). In youth prepare for age.

data.verse_text: Many plans are in a man’s heart, but the purpose of the LORD will prevail.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 16:1, Pro 16:9; Psa 33:10-11). The failure of man's devices is implied.

data.verse_text: The desire of a man is loving devotion; better to be poor than a liar.
data.commentary_text: desire--that is, to do good, indicates a kind disposition (Pro 11:23); and the poor thus affected are better than liars, who say and do not.

data.verse_text: The fear of the LORD leads to life, that one may rest content, without visitation from harm.
data.commentary_text:
The fear . . . life--(Compare Pro 3:2). 

abide--or, "remain contented" (Ti1 4:8). 

not visited with evil-- (Pro 10:3; Psa 37:25), as a judgment, in which sense visit is often used (Psa 89:32; Jer 6:15).

data.verse_text: The slacker buries his hand in the dish; he will not even bring it back to his mouth.
data.commentary_text: bosom--literally, a wide dish in which the hand was plunged in eating (Mat 26:23). Compare Pro 26:15, the sentiment expressed with equal irony and less exaggeration.

data.verse_text: Strike a mocker, and the simple will beware; rebuke the discerning man, and he will gain knowledge.
data.commentary_text: Such is the benefit of reproof; even the simple profit, much more the wise.

data.verse_text: He who assaults his father or evicts his mother is a son who brings shame and disgrace.
data.commentary_text: Unfilial conduct often condemned (Pro 17:21-25; Pro 20:20; Deu 21:18, Deu 21:21).

data.verse_text: If you cease to hear instruction, my son, you will stray from the words of knowledge.
data.commentary_text: Avoid whatever leads from truth.

data.verse_text: A corrupt witness mocks justice, and a wicked mouth swallows iniquity.
data.commentary_text:
ungodly witness--(Compare Margin), one false by bad principles (compare Pro 6:12). 

scorneth judgment--sets at naught the dictates of justice. 

devoureth--literally, "swalloweth," as something delightful.

data.verse_text: Judgments are prepared for mockers, and beatings for the backs of fools.
data.commentary_text:
Their punishment is sure, fixed, and ready (compare Pro 3:34; Pro 10:13).




Next: Proverbs Chapter 20

data.verse_text: Wine is a mocker, strong drink is a brawler, and whoever is led astray by them is not wise.
data.commentary_text:
(Pro. 20:1-30)

mocker--scorner. Such men are made by wine. 

strong drink--made by spicing wine (compare Isa 5:11, Isa 5:22); and it may include wine. 

raging--or boisterous as a drunkard. 

deceived--literally, "erring," or reeling.

data.verse_text: The terror of a king is like the roar of a lion; whoever provokes him forfeits his own life.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 19:12). Men who resist authority injure themselves (Rom 13:2).

data.verse_text: It is honorable for a man to resolve a dispute, but any fool will quarrel.
data.commentary_text:
to cease from strife--or, better, "to dwell from or without strife," denoting the habit of life. 

fool . . . meddling-- (Pro 17:14).

data.verse_text: The slacker does not plow in season; at harvest time he looks, but nothing is there.
data.commentary_text: shall . . . beg--literally, "ask" (in this sense, Psa 109:10).

data.verse_text: The intentions of a man’s heart are deep waters, but a man of understanding draws them out.
data.commentary_text: Counsel . . . water--that is, deeply hidden (Pro 18:4; Psa 13:2). The wise can discern well.

data.verse_text: Many a man proclaims his loving devotion, but who can find a trustworthy man?
data.commentary_text:
Boasters are unreliable. 

goodness--or, "kind disposition."

data.verse_text: The righteous man walks with integrity; blessed are his children after him.
data.commentary_text: The conduct of good men proclaims their sound principles. God's covenant and their good example secure blessing to their children (Pro 4:26; Psa 112:1-2).

data.verse_text: A king who sits on a throne to judge sifts out all evil with his eyes.
data.commentary_text: As in Pro 14:35; Pro 16:10, Pro 16:15, this is the character of a good king, not of all kings.

data.verse_text: Who can say, “I have kept my heart pure; I am cleansed from my sin”?
data.commentary_text: The interrogation in the affirmative strengthens the implied negation (compare Job 15:14; Ecc 7:20).

data.verse_text: Differing weights and unequal measures — both are detestable to the LORD.
data.commentary_text: Various measures, implying that some are wrong (compare Pro 11:1; Pro 16:11).

data.verse_text: Even a young man is known by his actions— whether his conduct is pure and upright.
data.commentary_text: The conduct of children even is the best test of principle (compare Mat 7:16).

data.verse_text: Ears that hear and eyes that see— the LORD has made them both.
data.commentary_text: Hence, of course, God will know all you do (Psa 94:9).

data.verse_text: Do not love sleep, or you will grow poor; open your eyes, and you will have plenty of food.
data.commentary_text:
Activity and diligence contrasted with sloth (Pro 6:9; Pro 10:11). 

lest . . . poverty--literally, "be deprived of inheritance."

data.verse_text: “Worthless, worthless!” says the buyer, but on the way out, he gloats.
data.commentary_text: when . . . his way--implying that he goes about boasting of his bargains.

data.verse_text: There is an abundance of gold and rubies, but lips of knowledge are a rare treasure.
data.commentary_text: The contrast denotes the greater value of knowledge (compare Pro 3:14-16).

data.verse_text: Take the garment of the one who posts security for a stranger; get collateral if it is for a foreigner.
data.commentary_text:
Take his garment--implies severe exaction, justified by the surety's rashness. 

a strange woman--by some readings "strangers," but the former here, and in Pro 27:13, is allowable, and strengthens the sense. The debauchee is less reliable than the merely careless.

data.verse_text: Food gained by fraud is sweet to a man, but later his mouth is full of gravel.
data.commentary_text:
Bread . . . sweet--either as unlawfully (Pro 9:17) or easily obtained. 

mouth . . . gravel--well expresses the pain and grief given at last.

data.verse_text: Set plans by consultation, and wage war under sound guidance.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 15:22). Be careful and considerate in important plans.

data.verse_text: He who reveals secrets is a constant gossip; avoid the one who babbles with his lips.
data.commentary_text:
Those who love to tell news will hardly keep secrets. 

flattereth . . . lips--(compare Margin; Pro 1:10). 

meddle . . . him--literally, "join," or "associate with."

data.verse_text: Whoever curses his father or mother, his lamp will be extinguished in deepest darkness.
data.commentary_text: his lamp--(Compare Pro 13:9; Pro 24:20).

data.verse_text: An inheritance gained quickly will not be blessed in the end.
data.commentary_text: gotten hastily--contrary to God's providence (Pro 28:20), implying its unjust or easy attainment; hence the man is punished, or spends freely what he got easily (compare Pro 20:17).

data.verse_text: Do not say, “I will avenge this evil!” Wait on the LORD, and He will save you.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Psa 27:14; Rom 12:17-19).

data.verse_text: Unequal weights are detestable to the LORD, and dishonest scales are no good.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 20:10; Pro 11:1).

data.verse_text: A man’s steps are from the LORD, so how can anyone understand his own way?
data.commentary_text:
Man's goings--literally, "Stately steppings of a strong man." 

a man--any common man. 

understand--or, "perceive."

data.verse_text: It is a trap for a man to dedicate something rashly, only later to reconsider his vows.
data.commentary_text: devoureth . . . holy--or, better, "who rashly speaks promises," or "devotes what is holy," consecrating any thing. This suits better the last clause, which expresses a similar view of the results of rashly vowing.

data.verse_text: A wise king separates out the wicked and drives the threshing wheel over them.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 20:8). 

bringeth . . . over them--The wheel was used for threshing grain. The figure denotes severity (compare Amo 1:3).

data.verse_text: The spirit of a man is the lamp of the LORD, searching out his inmost being.
data.commentary_text: The spirit . . . Lord--Men's minds are God's gifts, and thus able to search one another (compare Pro 20:5; Pro 18:8, Pro 18:17; Co1 2:11).

data.verse_text: Loving devotion and faithfulness preserve a king; by these he maintains his throne.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 3:3; Pro 16:6, Pro 16:12).

data.verse_text: The glory of young men is their strength, and gray hair is the splendor of the old.
data.commentary_text: The glory of young men . . . the beauty of old men--Each age has its peculiar excellence (Pro 16:31).

data.verse_text: Lashes and wounds scour evil, and beatings cleanse the inmost parts.
data.commentary_text:
blueness--literally, "joining," the process of uniting the edges of a wound throws off purulent matter. 

stripes . . . belly--So punishment provides healing of soul (Pro 18:8), by deterring from evil courses.




Next: Proverbs Chapter 21

data.verse_text: The king’s heart is a waterway in the hand of the LORD; He directs it where He pleases.
data.commentary_text:
(Pro. 21:1-31)

rivers--irrigating channels (Psa 1:3), whose course was easily turned (compare Deu 11:10). God disposes even kings as He pleases (Pro 16:9; Psa 33:15).

data.verse_text: All a man’s ways seem right to him, but the LORD weighs the heart.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 14:2; Pro. 16:2-25).

data.verse_text: To do righteousness and justice is more desirable to the LORD than sacrifice.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Psa 50:7-15; Isa 1:11, Isa 1:17).

data.verse_text: Haughty eyes and a proud heart— the guides of the wicked—are sin.
data.commentary_text:
high look--(Compare Margin; Psa 131:1). 

proud heart--or, "heart of breadth," one that is swollen (compare Psa 101:5). 

ploughing--better "lamp," a frequent figure for prosperity (Pro 20:20); hence joy or delight.

data.verse_text: The plans of the diligent bring plenty, as surely as haste leads to poverty.
data.commentary_text: The contrast is between steady industry and rashness (compare Pro 19:2).

data.verse_text: Making a fortune by a lying tongue is a vanishing mist, a deadly pursuit.
data.commentary_text:
The getting--or, "what is obtained" (compare Job 7:2; Jer 22:13, Hebrew). 

vanity . . . to and fro--as fleeting as chaff or stubble in the wind (compare Pro 20:17-21; Psa 62:10). Such gettings are unsatisfactory. 

them . . . death--act as if they did (Pro 8:36; Pro 17:19).

data.verse_text: The violence of the wicked will sweep them away because they refuse to do what is just.
data.commentary_text:
robbery--or, "destruction," especially oppression, of which they are authors. 

shall destroy--literally, "cut with a saw" (Kg1 7:9), that is, utterly ruin them. Their sins shall be visited on them in kind. 

to do judgment--what is just and right.

data.verse_text: The way of a guilty man is crooked, but the conduct of the innocent is upright.
data.commentary_text: of man--any one; his way is opposed to truth, and also estranged from it. The pure proves himself such by his right conduct.

data.verse_text: Better to live on a corner of the roof than to share a house with a quarrelsome wife.
data.commentary_text:
corner--a turret or arbor on the roof. 

brawling--or contentious. 

wide house--literally, "house of fellowship," large enough for several families.

data.verse_text: The soul of the wicked man craves evil; his neighbor finds no favor in his eyes.
data.commentary_text: So strongly does he desire to do evil (Psa 10:3; Ecc 8:11), that he will not even spare his friend if in his way.

data.verse_text: When a mocker is punished, the simple gain wisdom; and when a wise man is instructed, he acquires knowledge.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 19:25). That which the simple learn by the terrors of punishment, the wise learn by teaching.

data.verse_text: The Righteous One considers the house of the wicked and brings the wicked to ruin.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Psa 37:35-38; Psa 73:17, Psa 73:20). 

house--family or interests. 

overthroweth--either supply "God" (compare Pro 10:24), or the word is used impersonally.

data.verse_text: Whoever shuts his ears to the cry of the poor, he too shall cry out and receive no answer.
data.commentary_text: The principles of retribution, often taught (compare Psa 18:26; Mat 7:1-12).

data.verse_text: A gift in secret soothes anger, and a covert bribe pacifies great wrath.
data.commentary_text: The effect of bribery (Pro 17:23) is enhanced by secrecy, as the bribed person does not wish his motives made known.

data.verse_text: Justice executed is a joy to the righteous, but a terror to the workers of iniquity.
data.commentary_text: But the just love right and need no bribes. The wicked at last meet destruction, though for a time happy in concealing corruption.

data.verse_text: The man who strays from the path of understanding will rest in the assembly of the dead.
data.commentary_text:
the way of understanding--(Compare Pro 12:26; Pro 14:22). 

remain--that is, rest as at a journey's end; death will be his unchanging home.

data.verse_text: He who loves pleasure will become poor; the one who loves wine and oil will never be rich.
data.commentary_text: Costly luxuries impoverish.

data.verse_text: The wicked become a ransom for the righteous, and the faithless for the upright.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 11:8). By suffering what they had devised for the righteous, or brought on them, the wicked became their ransom, in the usual sense of substitutes (compare Jos 7:26; Est 7:9).

data.verse_text: Better to live in the desert than with a contentious and ill-tempered wife.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 21:9). 

wilderness--pasture, though uninhabitable ground (Psa 65:12).

data.verse_text: Precious treasures and oil are in the dwelling of the wise, but a foolish man consumes them.
data.commentary_text:
The wise, by diligence and care, lay up and increase wealth, while fools 

spend--literally, "swallow it up," greedily.

data.verse_text: He who pursues righteousness and loving devotion finds life, righteousness, and honor.
data.commentary_text: He who tries to act justly and kindly (Psa 34:14) will prosper and obtain justice and honor.

data.verse_text: A wise man scales the city of the mighty and pulls down the stronghold in which they trust.
data.commentary_text:
"Wisdom is better than strength" (Ecc 7:19; Ecc 9:15). 

strength . . . thereof--that in which they confide.

data.verse_text: He who guards his mouth and tongue keeps his soul from distress.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 13:2-3; Jam 3:6-10).

data.verse_text: Mocker is the name of the proud and arrogant man— of him who acts with excessive pride.
data.commentary_text: The reproachful name is deserved by those who treat others with anger and contempt.

data.verse_text: The craving of the slacker kills him because his hands refuse to work.
data.commentary_text: desire--that is, of ease and idleness brings him to starvation.

data.verse_text: All day long he covets more, but the righteous give without restraint.
data.commentary_text: The sin of covetousness marks the sluggard, as the virtue of benevolence the righteous.

data.verse_text: The sacrifice of the wicked is detestable— how much more so when brought with ill intent!
data.commentary_text:
God regards the heart, and hypocrisy is more odious than open inconsistency. 

wicked mind--or, "design" (Pro 1:4).

data.verse_text: A lying witness will perish, but the man who listens to truth will speak forever.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 19:5). 

that heareth--or heeds instruction, and so grows wise. 

speaketh constantly--or sincerely (compare Hab 1:5), and hence is believed (Pro 12:19; Jam 1:19).

data.verse_text: A wicked man hardens his face, but the upright man makes his way sure.
data.commentary_text:
hardeneth his face--is obstinate. 

directeth . . . way--considers it, and acts advisedly.

data.verse_text: There is no wisdom, no understanding, no counsel that can prevail against the LORD. A horse is prepared for the day of battle, but victory is of the LORD.
data.commentary_text:
Men's best devices and reliances are vain compared with God's, or without His aid (Pro 19:21; Psa 20:7; Psa 33:17).




Next: Proverbs Chapter 22

data.verse_text: A good name is more desirable than great riches; favor is better than silver and gold.
data.commentary_text:
(Pro. 22:1-29)

A good name-- (Job 30:8, Hebrew); "good" is supplied here from Ecc 7:1. 

loving favour--kind regard, that is, of the wise and good.

data.verse_text: The rich and the poor have this in common: The LORD is Maker of them all.
data.commentary_text: Before God all are on the same footing (Pro 14:31; Pro 17:5).

data.verse_text: The prudent see danger and take cover, but the simple keep going and suffer the consequences.
data.commentary_text: are punished--that is, for their temerity; for the evil is not necessarily punitive, as the prudent might otherwise be its objects.

data.verse_text: The rewards of humility and the fear of the LORD are wealth and honor and life.
data.commentary_text: humility and the fear of the Lord--are in apposition; one produces the other. On the results, compare Pro 3:16; Pro 8:18.

data.verse_text: Thorns and snares lie on the path of the perverse; he who guards his soul stays far from them.
data.commentary_text: he that . . . them--Those who properly watch over their own souls are thus preserved from the dangers which attend the way of perverse men (Pro 16:17).

data.verse_text: Train up a child in the way he should go, and when he is old he will not depart from it.
data.commentary_text:
Train--initiate, or early instruct. 

the way--literally, "his way," that selected for him in which he should go; for early training secures habitual walking in it.

data.verse_text: The rich rule over the poor, and the borrower is slave to the lender.
data.commentary_text: The influence of wealth sets aside moral distinctions is implied, and, of course, disapproved (compare Pro 19:6; Pro 21:14, &c.).

data.verse_text: He who sows injustice will reap disaster, and the rod of his fury will be destroyed.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 11:18; Psa 109:16-20; Gal 6:7-8). 

the rod . . . fail--His power to do evil will be destroyed.

data.verse_text: A generous man will be blessed, for he shares his bread with the poor.
data.commentary_text:
a bountiful eye--that is, a beneficent disposition. 

for he giveth . . . poor--His acts prove it.

data.verse_text: Drive out the mocker, and conflict will depart; even quarreling and insults will cease.
data.commentary_text: Cast out--or drive away. Scorners foster strife by taunts and revilings.

data.verse_text: He who loves a pure heart and gracious lips will have the king for a friend.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Margin). 

pureness of heart--and gentle, kind words win favor, even from kings.

data.verse_text: The LORD’s eyes keep watch over knowledge, but He frustrates the words of the faithless.
data.commentary_text:
preserve--or guard. 

knowledge--its principles and possessors. 

overthroweth--utterly confounds and destroys the wicked.

data.verse_text: The slacker says, “There is a lion outside! I will be slain in the streets!”
data.commentary_text: Frivolous excuses satisfy the indolent man's conscience.

data.verse_text: The mouth of an adulteress is a deep pit; he who is under the wrath of the LORD will fall into it.
data.commentary_text: The mouth--or flattering speeches (Pro 5:3; Pro 7:5) ensnare man, as pits, beasts. God makes their own sin their punishment.

data.verse_text: Foolishness is bound up in the heart of a child, but the rod of discipline drives it far from him.
data.commentary_text: is bound--or firmly fixed. Chastisement deters from crime and so leads to reformation of principle.

data.verse_text: Oppressing the poor to enrich oneself or giving gifts to the rich will surely lead to poverty.
data.commentary_text: These two vices pertain to the same selfish feeling. Both are deservedly odious to God and incur punishment.

data.verse_text: Incline your ear and hear the words of the wise— apply your mind to my knowledge—
data.commentary_text: Here begins another division of the book, marked by those encouragements to the pursuit of wisdom, which are found in the earlier chapters. It will be observed that at Pro. 22:22-24:12, the proverbs are generally expressed in two verses instead of one (see Introduction).

data.verse_text: for it is pleasing when you keep them within you and they are constantly on your lips.
data.commentary_text:
These lessons must be laid up in the mind, and 

fitted--or better, "fixed" in the lips so as to be ever ready.

data.verse_text: So that your trust may be in the LORD, I instruct you today—yes, you.
data.commentary_text: That . . . Lord--This is the design of the instruction.

data.verse_text: Have I not written for you thirty sayings about counsel and knowledge,
data.commentary_text:
excellent things--or probably of former times. 

counsels and knowledge--both advice and instruction.

data.verse_text: to show you true and reliable words, that you may soundly answer those who sent you?
data.commentary_text: Specially he desires to secure accuracy, so that his pupil may teach others.

data.verse_text: Do not rob a poor man because he is poor, and do not crush the afflicted at the gate, for the LORD will take up their case and will plunder those who rob them.
data.commentary_text:
Here follow ten precepts of two verses each. Though men fail to defend the poor, God will (Pro 17:5; Psa 12:5). 

in the gate--place of public gathering (Job 5:4; Psa 69:12).

data.verse_text: Do not make friends with an angry man, and do not associate with a hot-tempered man,
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 2:12-15; Pro 4:14).

data.verse_text: or you may learn his ways and entangle yourself in a snare.
data.commentary_text: a snare . . . soul--The unsuspecting are often misled by bad company.

data.verse_text: Do not be one who gives pledges, who puts up security for debts.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 6:1; Pro 17:18).

data.verse_text: If you have nothing with which to pay, why should your bed be taken from under you?
data.commentary_text: should he take, &c.--that is, the creditor.

data.verse_text: Do not move an ancient boundary stone which your fathers have placed.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 23:10). Do not entrench on others (Deu 19:14; Deu 27:17).

data.verse_text: Do you see a man skilled in his work? He will be stationed in the presence of kings; he will not stand before obscure men.
data.commentary_text:
Success rewards diligence (Pro 10:4; Pro 21:5).




Next: Proverbs Chapter 23

data.verse_text: When you sit down to dine with a ruler, consider carefully what is set before you,
data.commentary_text:
(Pro. 23:1-35)

Avoid the dangers of gluttony.

data.verse_text: and put a knife to your throat if you possess a great appetite.
data.commentary_text: put a knife--an Eastern figure for putting restraint on the appetite.

data.verse_text: Do not crave his delicacies, for that food is deceptive.
data.commentary_text: are deceitful meat--though well tasted, injurious.

data.verse_text: Do not wear yourself out to get rich; be wise enough to restrain yourself.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Ti1 6:9-10). 

thine own wisdom--which regards riches intrinsically as a blessing.

data.verse_text: When you glance at wealth, it disappears, for it makes wings for itself and flies like an eagle to the sky.
data.commentary_text: Wilt . . . eyes--As the eyes fly after or seek riches, they are not, that is, either become transitory or unsatisfying; fully expressed by their flying away.

data.verse_text: Do not eat the bread of a stingy man, and do not crave his delicacies; for he is keeping track, inwardly counting the cost. “Eat and drink,” he says to you, but his heart is not with you.
data.commentary_text:
Beware of deceitful men, whose courtesies even you will repent of having accepted. 

evil eye--or purpose (Pro 22:9; Deu 15:9; Mat 6:23).

data.verse_text: You will vomit up what little you have eaten and waste your pleasant words.
data.commentary_text: The morsel . . . words--that is, disgusted with his true character, all pleasant intercourse will be destroyed.

data.verse_text: Do not speak to a fool, for he will despise the wisdom of your words.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 9:8). "Cast not your pearls before swine" (Mat 7:6).

data.verse_text: Do not move an ancient boundary stone or encroach on the fields of the fatherless,
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 22:22-23).

data.verse_text: for their Redeemer is strong; He will take up their case against you.
data.commentary_text:
redeemer--or avenger (Lev 25:25-26; Num 35:12), hence advocate (Job 19:25). 

plead . . . thee--(Compare Job 31:21; Psa 35:1; Psa 68:5).

data.verse_text: Apply your heart to instruction and your ears to words of knowledge.
data.commentary_text: Here begins another series of precepts.

data.verse_text: Do not withhold discipline from a child; although you strike him with a rod, he will not die. Strike him with a rod, and you will deliver his soul from Sheol.
data.commentary_text: While there is little danger that the use of the "divine ordinance of the rod" will produce bodily harm, there is great hope of spiritual good.

data.verse_text: My son, if your heart is wise, my own heart will indeed rejoice.
data.commentary_text: The pleasure afforded the teacher by the pupil's progress is a motive to diligence.

data.verse_text: My inmost being will rejoice when your lips speak what is right.
data.commentary_text: my reins--(Compare Psa 7:9).

data.verse_text: Do not let your heart envy sinners, but always continue in the fear of the LORD.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Margin). The prosperity of the wicked is short.

data.verse_text: For surely there is a future, and your hope will not be cut off.
data.commentary_text: an end--or, "hereafter," another time, when apparent inequalities shall be adjusted (compare Psa 37:28-38).

data.verse_text: Listen, my son, and be wise, and guide your heart on the right course.
data.commentary_text: guide . . . way--or direct thy thoughts to a right course of conduct (compare Pro 4:4; Pro 9:6).

data.verse_text: Do not join those who drink too much wine or gorge themselves on meat.
data.commentary_text: riotous . . . flesh--prodigal, or eating more than necessary. Instead of "their flesh" (compare Margin), better, "flesh to them," that is, used for pleasure.

data.verse_text: For the drunkard and the glutton will come to poverty, and drowsiness will clothe them in rags.
data.commentary_text: drowsiness--the dreamy sleep of the slothful.

data.verse_text: Listen to your father who gave you life, and do not despise your mother when she is old.
data.commentary_text:
Hearken--that is, obey (Pro 1:8; Eph 6:1). 

despise . . . old--Adults revere the parents whom, as children, they once obeyed.

data.verse_text: Invest in truth and never sell it— in wisdom and instruction and understanding.
data.commentary_text:
Buy--literally, "get" (Pro 4:5). 

truth--generally and specially as opposed to errors of all kinds.

data.verse_text: The father of a righteous man will greatly rejoice, and he who fathers a wise son will delight in him. May your father and mother be glad, and may she who gave you birth rejoice!
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 10:1; Pro 17:21, Pro 17:25).

data.verse_text: My son, give me your heart, and let your eyes delight in my ways.
data.commentary_text:
A solemn warning against whoredom and drunkenness (Hos 4:11). 

give me--This is the address of that divine wisdom so often presented (Pro 8:1; Pro 9:3, &c.). 

heart--confidence. 

observe--keep. 

my ways--such as I teach you (Pro 3:17; Pro 9:6).

data.verse_text: For a prostitute is a deep pit, and an adulteress is a narrow well.
data.commentary_text:
deep ditch--a narrow pit, out of which it is hard to climb. 

lieth in wait--to ensnare men into the pit, as hunters entrap game (compare Pro 22:14).

data.verse_text: Like a robber she lies in wait and multiplies the faithless among men.
data.commentary_text: increaseth . . . transgressors-- (Pro 5:8-10). The vice alluded to is peculiarly hardening to the heart.

data.verse_text: Who has woe? Who has sorrow? Who has contentions? Who has complaints? Who has needless wounds? Who has bloodshot eyes? Those who linger over wine, who go to taste mixed drinks.
data.commentary_text:
This picture is often sadly realized now. 

mixed wine--(Compare Pro 9:2; Isa 5:11).

data.verse_text: Do not gaze at wine while it is red, when it sparkles in the cup and goes down smoothly.
data.commentary_text:
when . . . red--the color denoting greater strength (compare Gen 49:11; Deu 32:14). 

giveth . . . cup--literally, "gives its eye," that is, sparkles. 

moveth . . . aright--Perhaps its foaming is meant.

data.verse_text: In the end it bites like a snake and stings like a viper.
data.commentary_text: The acute miseries resulting from drunkenness contrasted with the temptations.

data.verse_text: Your eyes will see strange things, and your mind will utter perversities. You will be like one sleeping on the high seas or lying on the top of a mast:
data.commentary_text: The moral effects: it inflames passion (Gen 19:31, Gen 19:35), lays open the heart, produces insensibility to the greatest dangers, and debars from reformation, under the severest sufferings.

data.verse_text: “They struck me, but I feel no pain! They beat me, but I did not know it! When can I wake up to search for another drink?”
data.commentary_text:
awake--that is, from drunkenness (Gen 9:24). This is the language rather of acts than of the tongue.




Next: Proverbs Chapter 24

data.verse_text: Do not envy wicked men or desire their company;
data.commentary_text:
(Pro. 24:1-34)

(Compare Pro 23:3, Pro 23:17; Psa 37:1).

data.verse_text: for their hearts devise violence, and their lips declare trouble.
data.commentary_text:
studieth--meditateth. 

talk . . . mischief--Their expressed purposes are to do evil.

data.verse_text: By wisdom a house is built and by understanding it is established;
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 14:1; Isa 54:14). 

house--including the family.

data.verse_text: through knowledge its rooms are filled with every precious and beautiful treasure.
data.commentary_text: by knowledge . . . riches-- (Pro 8:18; Pro 21:20).

data.verse_text: A wise man is strong, and a man of knowledge enhances his strength. Only with sound guidance should you wage war, and victory lies in a multitude of counselors.
data.commentary_text: The general statement (Ecc 9:16, Ecc 9:18) is specially illustrated (compare Pro 21:22; Psa 144:1).

data.verse_text: Wisdom is too high for a fool; he does not open his mouth in the meeting place.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 14:16). 

in the gate--(Compare Pro 22:22).

data.verse_text: He who plots evil will be called a schemer.
data.commentary_text: So called even if he fails to do evil.

data.verse_text: A foolish scheme is sin, and a mocker is detestable to men.
data.commentary_text: Same thought varied.

data.verse_text: If you faint in the day of distress, how small is your strength!
data.commentary_text: Literally, "If thou fail in the day of straits (adversity), strait (or, small) is thy strength," which is then truly tested.
