data.verse_text: Rescue those being led away to death, and restrain those stumbling toward the slaughter. If you say, “Behold, we did not know about this,” does not He who weighs hearts consider it? Does not the One who guards your life know? Will He not repay a man according to his deeds?
data.commentary_text:
Neglect of known duty is sin (Jam 4:17). 

ready--literally, "bowing down" 

to be slain--that is, unjustly. God's retributive justice cannot be avoided by professed ignorance.

data.verse_text: Eat honey, my son, for it is good, and the honeycomb is sweet to your taste.
data.commentary_text: As delicious food whets the appetite, so should the rewards of wisdom excite us to seek it.

data.verse_text: Know therefore that wisdom is sweet to your soul. If you find it, there is a future for you, and your hope will never be cut off.
data.commentary_text: reward--literally, "after part," the proper result (compare Pro 23:18; Psa 37:37-38).

data.verse_text: Do not lie in wait, O wicked man, near the dwelling of the righteous; do not destroy his resting place.
data.commentary_text: The plots of the wicked against the good, though partially, shall not be fully successful (Psa 37:24); while the wicked, falling under penal evil, find no help.

data.verse_text: For though a righteous man may fall seven times, he still gets up; but the wicked stumble in bad times.
data.commentary_text: seven times--often, or many (Pro 6:16, Pro 6:31; Pro 9:1).

data.verse_text: Do not gloat when your enemy falls, and do not let your heart rejoice when he stumbles, or the LORD will see and disapprove, and turn His wrath away from him.
data.commentary_text: Yet let none rejoice over the fate of evildoers, lest God punish their wrong spirit by relieving the sufferer (compare Pro 17:5; Job 31:29).

data.verse_text: Do not fret over evildoers, and do not be envious of the wicked.
For the evil man has no future; the lamp of the wicked will be extinguished.
data.commentary_text: candle--or, "prosperity"; it shall come to an end (Pro 13:9; Pro 20:20).

data.verse_text: My son, fear the LORD and the king, and do not associate with the rebellious.
data.commentary_text:
A warning against impiety and resistance to lawful rule (Rom 13:1-7; Pe1 2:17). 

meddle . . . change--(Compare Margin), literally, "mingle not yourself," avoid the society of restless persons.

data.verse_text: For they will bring sudden destruction. Who knows what ruin they can bring?
data.commentary_text: their calamity, &c.--either what God and the king inflict, or what changers and their company suffer; better the first.

data.verse_text: These also are sayings of the wise: To show partiality in judgment is not good.
data.commentary_text:
These . . . wise--literally, "are of the wise," as authors (compare "Psalms of David," Hebrew). "These" refers to the verses following, Pro 24:24-34. 

to have respect--literally, "to discern faces," show partiality,

data.verse_text: Whoever tells the guilty, “You are innocent”— peoples will curse him, and nations will denounce him; but it will go well with those who convict the guilty, and rich blessing will come upon them.
data.commentary_text: of which an example is justifying the wicked, to which is opposed, rebuking him, which has a blessing.

data.verse_text: An honest answer given is like a kiss on the lips.
data.commentary_text:
kiss his lips--love and obey, do homage (Psa 2:12; Sol 8:1). 

right answer--literally, "plain words" (compare Pro 8:9), opposed to deceptive, or obscure.

data.verse_text: Complete your outdoor work and prepare your field; after that, you may build your house.
data.commentary_text: Prepare . . . in the field--Secure, by diligence, a proper support, and then build; provide necessaries, then comforts, to which a house rather pertained, in a mild climate, permitting the use of tents.

data.verse_text: Do not testify against your neighbor without cause, and do not deceive with your lips.
data.commentary_text: Do not speak even truth needlessly against any, and never falsehood.

data.verse_text: Do not say, “I will do to him as he has done to me; I will repay the man according to his work.”
data.commentary_text: Especially avoid retaliation (Mat 5:43-45; Rom 12:17).

data.verse_text: I went past the field of a slacker and by the vineyard of a man lacking judgment. Thorns had grown up everywhere, thistles had covered the ground, and the stone wall was broken down.
data.commentary_text: A striking picture of the effects of sloth.

data.verse_text: I observed and took it to heart; I looked and received instruction: A little sleep, a little slumber, a little folding of the hands to rest, and poverty will come upon you like a robber, and need like a bandit.
data.commentary_text:
From the folly of the sluggard learn wisdom (Pro 6:10-11).




Next: Proverbs Chapter 25

data.verse_text: These are additional proverbs of Solomon, which were copied by the men of Hezekiah king of Judah:
data.commentary_text:
(Pro. 25:1-28)

The character of these proverbs sustains the title (see Introduction). 

also--refers to the former part of the book. 

copied out--literally, "transferred," that is, from some other book to this; not given from memory.

data.verse_text: It is the glory of God to conceal a matter and the glory of kings to search it out.
data.commentary_text: God's unsearchableness impresses us with awe (compare Isa 45:15; Rom 11:33). But kings, being finite, should confer with wise counsellors;

data.verse_text: As the heavens are high and the earth is deep, so the hearts of kings cannot be searched.
data.commentary_text: Ye wisely keeping state secrets, which to common men are as inaccessible heights and depths.

data.verse_text: Remove the dross from the silver, and a vessel for a silversmith will come forth.
data.commentary_text: As separating impurities from ore leaves pure silver, so taking from a king wicked counsellors leaves a wise and beneficent government.

data.verse_text: Remove the wicked from the king’s presence, and his throne will be established in righteousness.
data.commentary_text: before--or, "in presence of," as courtiers stood about a king.

data.verse_text: Do not exalt yourself in the presence of the king, and do not stand in the place of great men; for it is better that he says to you, “Come up here!” than that you should be demoted in the presence of the prince. Even what you have seen with your own eyes,
data.commentary_text: Do not intrude into the presence of the king, for the elevation of the humble is honorable, but the humbling of the proud disgraceful (Luk 14:8-10).

data.verse_text: do not bring hastily to court. Otherwise, what will you do in the end when your neighbor puts you to shame?
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 3:30). 

lest . . . shame--lest you do what you ought not, when shamed by defeat, or "lest thou art shut out from doing any thing."

data.verse_text: Argue your case with your neighbor without betraying another’s confidence, lest the one who hears may disgrace you, and your infamy never go away.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Mat 5:25, Margin). 

secret--that is, of your opponent, for his disadvantage, and so you be disgraced, not having discussed your difficulties with him.

data.verse_text: A word fitly spoken is like apples of gold in settings of silver.
data.commentary_text:
a word fitly--literally, "quickly," as wheels roll, just in time. The comparison as apples . . . silver gives a like sense. 

apples, &c.--either real apples of golden color, in a silver network basket, or imitations on silver embroidery.

data.verse_text: Like an earring of gold or an ornament of fine gold is a wise man’s rebuke to a listening ear.
data.commentary_text: Those who desire to know and do rightly, most highly esteem good counsel (Pro 9:9; Pro 15:31). The listening ear is better than one hung with gold.

data.verse_text: Like the cold of snow in the time of harvest is a trustworthy messenger to those who send him; he refreshes the soul of his masters.
data.commentary_text: Snow from mountains was used to cool drinks; so refreshing is a faithful messenger (Pro 13:17).

data.verse_text: Like clouds and wind without rain is the man who boasts of gifts never given.
data.commentary_text:
clouds--literally, "vapors" (Jer 10:13), clouds only in appearance. 

a false gift--promised, but not given.

data.verse_text: Through patience a ruler can be persuaded, and a gentle tongue can break a bone.
data.commentary_text:
Gentleness and kindness overcome the most powerful and obstinate. 

long forbearing--or, "slowness to anger" (Pro 14:29; Pro 15:18).

data.verse_text: If you find honey, eat just what you need, lest you have too much and vomit it up. Seldom set foot in your neighbor’s house, lest he grow weary and hate you.
data.commentary_text: A comparison, as a surfeit of honey produces physical disgust, so your company, however agreeable in moderation, may, if excessive, lead your friend to hate you.

data.verse_text: Like a club or sword or sharp arrow is a man who bears false witness against his neighbor.
data.commentary_text:
A false witness is as destructive to reputation, as such weapons to the body (Pro 24:28). 

beareth . . . witness--literally, "answereth questions," as before a judge, against his neighbor.

data.verse_text: Like a broken tooth or a foot out of joint is confidence in a faithless man in time of trouble.
data.commentary_text: Treachery annoys as well as deceives.

data.verse_text: Like one who removes a garment on a cold day or vinegar poured on a wound is one who sings songs to a heavy heart.
data.commentary_text: Not only is the incongruity of songs (that is, joyful) and sadness meant, but an accession of sadness, by want of sympathy, is implied.

data.verse_text: If your enemy is hungry, give him food to eat, and if he is thirsty, give him water to drink. For in so doing, you will heap burning coals on his head, and the LORD will reward you.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Mat 5:44; Rom 12:20). As metals are melted by heaping coals upon them, so is the heart softened by kindness.

data.verse_text: As the north wind brings forth rain, so a backbiting tongue brings angry looks.
data.commentary_text: Better, "As the north wind bringeth forth (Psa 90:2) or produces rain, so does a concealed or slandering tongue produce anger."

data.verse_text: Better to live on a corner of the roof than to share a house with a quarrelsome wife.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 21:9, Pro 21:19).

data.verse_text: Like cold water to a weary soul is good news from a distant land.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 25:13). 

good news--that is, of some loved interest or absent friend, the more grateful as coming from afar.

data.verse_text: Like a muddied spring or a polluted well is a righteous man who gives way to the wicked.
data.commentary_text: From troubled fountains and corrupt springs no healthy water is to be had, so when the righteous are oppressed by the wicked, their power for good is lessened or destroyed.

data.verse_text: It is not good to eat too much honey or to search out one’s own glory.
data.commentary_text:
Satiety surfeits (Pro 25:16); so men who are self-glorious find shame. 

is not glory--"not" is supplied from the first clause, or "is grievous," in which sense a similar word is used (Pro 27:2).

data.verse_text: Like a city whose walls are broken down is a man who does not control his temper.
data.commentary_text:
Such are exposed to the incursions of evil thoughts and successful temptations.




Next: Proverbs Chapter 26

data.verse_text: Like snow in summer and rain at harvest, honor does not befit a fool.
data.commentary_text:
(Pro. 26:1-28)

The incongruities of nature illustrate also those of the moral world. The fool's unworthiness is also implied (Pro 17:7; Pro 19:10).

data.verse_text: Like a fluttering sparrow or darting swallow, an undeserved curse does not come to rest.
data.commentary_text:
Though not obvious to us, 

the bird--literally, "sparrow"--and 

swallow--have an object in their motions, so penal evil falls on none without a reason.

data.verse_text: A whip for the horse, a bridle for the donkey, and a rod for the backs of fools!
data.commentary_text: The rod is as much needed by fools and as well suited to them, as whips and bridles are for beasts.

data.verse_text: Do not answer a fool according to his folly, or you yourself will be like him.
data.commentary_text: Answer not--that is, approvingly by like folly.

data.verse_text: Answer a fool according to his folly, lest he become wise in his own eyes.
data.commentary_text: Answer--by reproof.

data.verse_text: Like cutting off one’s own feet or drinking violence is the sending of a message by the hand of a fool.
data.commentary_text:
A fool fails by folly as surely as if he were maimed. 

drinketh damage--that is, gets it abundantly (Job 15:16; Job 34:7).

data.verse_text: Like lame legs hanging limp is a proverb in the mouth of a fool.
data.commentary_text: legs . . . equal--or, "take away the legs," or "the legs . . . are weak." In any case the idea is that they are the occasion of an awkwardness, such as the fool shows in using a parable or proverb (see Introduction; Pro 17:7).

data.verse_text: Like binding a stone into a sling is the giving of honor to a fool.
data.commentary_text: A stone, bound in a sling, is useless; so honor, conferred on a fool, is thrown away.

data.verse_text: Like a thorn that falls into the hand of a drunkard is a proverb in the mouth of a fool.
data.commentary_text: As vexatious and unmanageable as a thorn in a drunkard's hand is a parable to a fool. He will be as apt to misuse is as to use it rightly.

data.verse_text: Like an archer who wounds at random is he who hires a fool or passerby.
data.commentary_text:
Various versions of this are proposed (compare Margin). Better perhaps--"Much He injures (or literally, "wounds") all who reward," &c., that is, society is injured by encouraging evil men. 

transgressors--may be rendered "vagrants." The word "God" is improperly supplied.

data.verse_text: As a dog returns to its vomit, so a fool repeats his folly.
data.commentary_text: returneth . . . folly--Though disgusting to others, the fool delights in his folly.

data.verse_text: Do you see a man who is wise in his own eyes? There is more hope for a fool than for him.
data.commentary_text: The self-conceited are taught with more difficulty than the stupid.

data.verse_text: The slacker says, “A lion is in the road! A fierce lion roams the public square!”
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 22:13).

data.verse_text: As a door turns on its hinges, so the slacker turns on his bed.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 6:10; Pro 24:33).

data.verse_text: The slacker buries his hand in the dish; it wearies him to bring it back to his mouth.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 19:24).

data.verse_text: The slacker is wiser in his own eyes than seven men who answer discreetly.
data.commentary_text: The thoughtless being ignorant of their ignorance are conceited.

data.verse_text: Like one who grabs a dog by the ears is a passerby who meddles in a quarrel not his own.
data.commentary_text: meddleth--as in Pro 20:19; Pro 24:21; as either holding a dog by the ears or letting him go involves danger, so success in another man's strife or failure involves a useless risk of reputation, does no good, and may do us harm.

data.verse_text: Like a madman shooting firebrands and deadly arrows, so is the man who deceives his neighbor and says, “I was only joking!”
data.commentary_text: Such are reckless of results.

data.verse_text: Without wood, a fire goes out; without gossip, a conflict ceases. Like charcoal for embers and wood for fire, so is a quarrelsome man for kindling strife.
data.commentary_text: The talebearers foster (Pro 16:28), and the contentious excite, strife.

data.verse_text: The words of a gossip are like choice morsels that go down into the inmost being.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 18:8).

data.verse_text: Like glaze covering an earthen vessel are burning lips and a wicked heart.
data.commentary_text: Warm professions can no more give value to insincerity than silver coating to rude earthenware.

data.verse_text: A hateful man disguises himself with his speech, but he lays up deceit in his heart.
data.commentary_text: dissembleth--though an unusual sense of the word (compare Margin), is allowable, and better suits the context, which sets forth hypocrisy.

data.verse_text: When he speaks graciously, do not believe him, for seven abominations fill his heart.
data.commentary_text:
Sentiment of Pro 26:24 carried out. 

seven abominations in his heart--that is, very many (compare Pro 24:16).

data.verse_text: Though his hatred is concealed by deception, his wickedness will be exposed in the assembly. He who digs a pit will fall into it, and he who rolls a stone will have it roll back on him.
data.commentary_text: Deceit will at last be exposed, and the wicked by their own arts often bring on retribution (compare Pro 12:13; Psa 7:16; Psa 9:17, &c.).

data.verse_text: A lying tongue hates those it crushes, and a flattering mouth causes ruin.
data.commentary_text:
Men hate those they injure. 

A lying tongue--"lips" for the persons (compare Pro 4:24; Psa 12:3).




Next: Proverbs Chapter 27

data.verse_text: Do not boast about tomorrow, for you do not know what a day may bring.
data.commentary_text:
(Pro. 27:1-27)

Do not confide implicitly in your plans (Pro 16:9; Pro 19:21; Jam 4:13-15).

data.verse_text: Let another praise you, and not your own mouth— a stranger, and not your own lips.
data.commentary_text: Avoid self-praise.

data.verse_text: A stone is heavy and sand is a burden, but aggravation from a fool outweighs them both.
data.commentary_text:
heavy--The literal sense of "heavy," applied to material subjects, illustrates its figurative, "grievous," applied to moral. 

a fool's wrath--is unreasonable and excessive.

data.verse_text: Wrath is cruel and anger is like a flood, but who can withstand jealousy?
data.commentary_text: envy--or, "jealousy" (compare Margin; Pro 6:34), is more unappeasable than the simpler bad passions.

data.verse_text: Better an open rebuke than love that is concealed. The wounds of a friend are faithful, but the kisses of an enemy are deceitful.
data.commentary_text: secret love--not manifested in acts is useless; and even, if its exhibition by rebukes wounds us, such love is preferable to the frequent (compare Margin), and hence deceitful, kisses of an enemy.

data.verse_text: The soul that is full loathes honey, but to a hungry soul, any bitter thing is sweet.
data.commentary_text: The luxury of wealth confers less happiness than the healthy appetite of labor.

data.verse_text: Like a bird that strays from its nest is a man who wanders from his home.
data.commentary_text: Such are not only out of place, but out of duty and in danger.

data.verse_text: Oil and incense bring joy to the heart, and the sweetness of a friend is counsel to the soul.
data.commentary_text:
rejoice the heart--the organ of perceiving what pleases the senses. 

sweetness . . . counsel--or, "wise counsel is also pleasing."

data.verse_text: Do not forsake your friend or your father’s friend, and do not go to your brother’s house in the day of your calamity; better a neighbor nearby than a brother far away.
data.commentary_text: Adhere to tried friends. The ties of blood may be less reliable than those of genuine friendship.

data.verse_text: Be wise, my son, and bring joy to my heart, so that I can answer him who taunts me.
data.commentary_text: The wisdom of children both reflects credit on parents and contributes to their aid in difficulties.

data.verse_text: The prudent see danger and take cover; but the simple keep going and pay the penalty. Take the garment of him who posts security for a stranger; get collateral if it is for a foreigner.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 20:16; Pro 22:3).

data.verse_text: If one blesses his neighbor with a loud voice early in the morning, it will be counted to him as a curse.
data.commentary_text: Excessive zeal in praising raises suspicions of selfishness.

data.verse_text: A constant dripping on a rainy day and a contentious woman are alike—
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 19:13). 

very . . . day--literally, "a day of showers."

data.verse_text: restraining her is like holding back the wind or grasping oil with one’s right hand.
data.commentary_text:
hideth--or, "restrains" (that is, tries to do it); is as fruitless an effort, as that of holding the wind. 

the ointment of his right hand--the organ of power (Psa 17:7; Psa 18:35). His right hand endeavors to repress perfume, but vainly. Some prefer: "His right hand comes on oil," that is, "cannot take hold." Such a woman cannot be tamed.

data.verse_text: As iron sharpens iron, so one man sharpens another.
data.commentary_text: a man sharpeneth . . . friend--that is, conversation promotes intelligence, which the face exhibits.

data.verse_text: Whoever tends a fig tree will eat its fruit, and he who looks after his master will be honored.
data.commentary_text: Diligence secures a reward, even for the humble servant.

data.verse_text: As water reflects the face, so the heart reflects the true man.
data.commentary_text: We may see our characters in the developed tempers of others.

data.verse_text: Sheol and Abaddon are never satisfied; so the eyes of man are never satisfied.
data.commentary_text: Men's cupidity is as insatiable as the grave.

data.verse_text: A crucible for silver and a furnace for gold, but a man is tested by the praise accorded him.
data.commentary_text:
Praise tests character. 

a man to his praise--according to his praise, as he bears it. Thus vain men seek it, weak men are inflated by it, wise men disregard it, &c.

data.verse_text: Though you grind a fool like grain with mortar and a pestle, yet his folly will not depart from him.
data.commentary_text: The obstinate wickedness of such is incurable by the heaviest inflictions.

data.verse_text: Be sure to know the state of your flocks, and pay close attention to your herds; for riches are not forever, nor does a crown endure to every generation.
data.commentary_text: flocks--constituted the staple of wealth. It is only by care and diligence that the most solid possessions can be perpetuated (Pro 23:5).

data.verse_text: When hay is removed and new growth appears and the grain from the hills is gathered, the lambs will provide you with clothing, and the goats with the price of a field.
data.commentary_text:
The fact that providential arrangements furnish the means of competence to those who properly use them is another motive to diligence (compare Psa 65:9-13). 

The hay appeareth--literally, "Grass appeareth" (Job 40:15; Psa 104:14).

data.verse_text: You will have plenty of goats’ milk to feed you— food for your household and nourishment for your maidservants.
data.commentary_text:
household--literally, "house," the family (Act 16:15; Co1 1:16).




Next: Proverbs Chapter 28

data.verse_text: The wicked flee when no one pursues, but the righteous are as bold as a lion.
data.commentary_text:
(Pro. 28:1-28)

A bad conscience makes men timid; the righteous are alone truly bold (Pro 14:26; Psa 27:1).

data.verse_text: A land in rebellion has many rulers, but a man of understanding and knowledge maintains order.
data.commentary_text:
Anarchy producing contending rulers shortens the reign of each. 

but by a man . . . prolonged--or, "by a man of understanding--that is, a good ruler--he who knows or regards the right, that is, a good citizen, shall prolong (his days)." Good rulers are a blessing to the people. Bad government as a punishment for evil is contrasted with good as blessing to the good.

data.verse_text: A destitute leader who oppresses the poor is like a driving rain that leaves no food.
data.commentary_text: A poor man, &c.--Such, in power, exact more severely, and so leave subjects bare.

data.verse_text: Those who forsake the law praise the wicked, but those who keep the law resist them.
data.commentary_text: They that forsake . . . wicked--Wrongdoers encourage one another.

data.verse_text: Evil men do not understand justice, but those who seek the LORD comprehend fully.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Joh 7:17). Ignorance of moral truth is due to unwillingness to know it.

data.verse_text: Better a poor man who walks with integrity than a rich man whose ways are perverse.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 10:6). Riches cannot compensate for sin, nor the want of them affect integrity.

data.verse_text: A discerning son keeps the law, but a companion of gluttons disgraces his father.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 17:25). 

riotous men--or, "gluttons" (Pro 23:20-21).

data.verse_text: He who increases his wealth by interest and usury lays it up for one who is kind to the poor.
data.commentary_text: usury . . . unjust gain--(Compare Margin). The two terms, meaning nearly the same, may denote excessive interest. God's providence directs the proper use of wealth.

data.verse_text: Whoever turns his ear away from hearing the law, even his prayer is detestable.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 15:8; Pro 21:27). 

hearing--that is, obeying. God requires sincere worshippers (Psa 66:18; Joh 4:24).

data.verse_text: He who leads the upright along the path of evil will fall into his own pit, but the blameless will inherit what is good.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 26:27).

data.verse_text: A rich man is wise in his own eyes, but a poor man with discernment sees through him.
data.commentary_text: A poor but wise man can discover (and expose) the rich and self-conceited.

data.verse_text: When the righteous triumph, there is great glory, but when the wicked rise, men hide themselves.
data.commentary_text:
great glory--or, cause for it to a people, for the righteous rejoice in good, and righteousness exalts a nation (Pro 14:34). 

a man . . . hidden--that is, the good retire, or all kinds try to escape a wicked rule.

data.verse_text: He who conceals his sins will not prosper, but whoever confesses and renounces them will find mercy.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Psa 32:3-5). Concealment of sin delivers none from God's wrath, but He shows mercy to the humble penitent (Psa 51:4).

data.verse_text: Blessed is the man who is always reverent, but he who hardens his heart falls into trouble.
data.commentary_text:
feareth--that is, God, and so repents. 

hardeneth his heart--makes himself insensible to sin, and so will not repent (Pro 14:16; Pro 29:1).

data.verse_text: Like a roaring lion or a charging bear is a wicked ruler over a helpless people.
data.commentary_text: The rapacity and cruelty of such beasts well represent some wicked men (compare Psa 7:2; Psa 17:12).

data.verse_text: A leader who lacks judgment is also a great oppressor, but he who hates dishonest profit will prolong his days.
data.commentary_text: The prince . . . understanding--that is, He does not perceive that oppression jeopards his success. Covetousness often produces oppression, hence the contrast.

data.verse_text: A man burdened by bloodguilt will flee into the Pit; let no one support him.
data.commentary_text:
doeth violence . . . blood, &c.--or, that is oppressed by the blood of life (Gen 9:6), which he has taken. 

to the pit--the grave or destruction (Pro 1:12; Job 33:18-24; Psa 143:7). 

stay him--sustain or deliver him.

data.verse_text: He who walks with integrity will be kept safe, but whoever is perverse in his ways will suddenly fall.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 10:9; Pro 17:20). Double dealing is eventually fatal.

data.verse_text: The one who works his land will have plenty of food, but whoever chases fantasies will have his fill of poverty.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 10:4; Pro 20:4). 

vain persons--idle, useless drones, implying that they are also wicked (Pro 12:11; Psa 26:4).

data.verse_text: A faithful man will abound with blessings, but one eager to be rich will not go unpunished.
data.commentary_text: maketh haste . . . rich--implying deceit or fraud (Pro 20:21), and so opposed to "faithful" or reliable.

data.verse_text: To show partiality is not good, yet a man will do wrong for a piece of bread.
data.commentary_text: respect of persons-- (Pro 24:23). Such are led to evil by the slightest motive.

data.verse_text: A stingy man hastens after wealth and does not know that poverty awaits him.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 28:20). 

evil eye--in the general sense of Pro 23:6, here more specific for covetousness (compare Pro 22:9; Mat 20:15). 

poverty . . . him--by God's providence.

data.verse_text: He who rebukes a man will later find more favor than one who flatters with his tongue.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 9:8-9; Pro 27:5). Those benefited by reproof will love their monitors.

data.verse_text: He who robs his father or mother, saying, “It is not wrong,” is a companion to the man who destroys.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Mat 15:4-6). Such, though heirs, are virtually thieves, to be ranked with highwaymen.

data.verse_text: A greedy man stirs up strife, but he who trusts in the LORD will prosper.
data.commentary_text:
of a proud heart--literally, "puffed up of soul"--that is, self-confident, and hence overbearing and litigious. 

made fat--or, "prosperous" (Pro 11:25; Pro 16:20).

data.verse_text: He who trusts in himself is a fool, but one who walks in wisdom will be safe.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 3:6-8). 

walketh wisely--that is, trusting in God (Pro 22:17-19).

data.verse_text: Whoever gives to the poor will not be in need, but he who hides his eyes will receive many curses.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 11:24-26). 

hideth his eyes--as the face (Psa 27:9; Psa 69:17), denotes inattention.

data.verse_text: When the wicked come to power, people hide themselves; but when they perish, the righteous flourish.
data.commentary_text:
The elevation of the wicked to power drives men to seek refuge from tyranny (compare Pro 28:12; Pro 11:10; Psa 12:8).




Next: Proverbs Chapter 29

data.verse_text: A man who remains stiff-necked after much reproof will suddenly be shattered beyond recovery.
data.commentary_text:
(Pro. 29:1-27)

hardeneth . . . neck--obstinately refuses counsel (Kg2 17:14; Neh 9:16). 

destroyed--literally, "shivered" or "utterly broken to pieces." 

without remedy--literally, "without healing" or repairing.

data.verse_text: When the righteous flourish, the people rejoice, but when the wicked rule, the people groan.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 11:10; Pro 28:28). 

in authority--(Compare Margin), increased in power.

data.verse_text: A man who loves wisdom brings joy to his father, but a companion of prostitutes squanders his wealth.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 4:6-7; Pro 10:1, &c.).

data.verse_text: By justice a king brings stability to the land, but a man who exacts tribute demolishes it.
data.commentary_text:
by judgment--that is, righteous decisions, opposed to those procured by gifts (compare Pro 28:21), by which good government is perverted. 

land--for nation.

data.verse_text: A man who flatters his neighbor spreads a net for his feet.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 26:28). 

spreadeth . . . feet--By misleading him as to his real character, the flatterer brings him to evil, prepared by himself or others.

data.verse_text: An evil man is caught by his own sin, but a righteous one sings and rejoices.
data.commentary_text:
In--or, "By" 

the transgression--he is brought into difficulty (Pro 12:13), but the righteous go on prospering, and so sing or rejoice.

data.verse_text: The righteous consider the cause of the poor, but the wicked have no regard for such concerns.
data.commentary_text:
considereth--literally, "knows," as Psa 1:6. 

the cause--that is, in courts of justice (compare Pro 29:14). The voluntary neglect of it by the wicked (Pro 28:27) occasions oppression.

data.verse_text: Mockers inflame a city, but the wise turn away anger.
data.commentary_text:
Scornful men--those who contemptuously disregard God's law. 

bring--(Compare Margin), kindle strife. 

turn away wrath--that is, "abate wrath."

data.verse_text: If a wise man goes to court with a fool, there will be raving and laughing with no resolution.
data.commentary_text:
contendeth--that is, in law. 

whether . . . laugh--The fool, whether angry or good-humored, is unsettled; or referring the words to the wise man, the sense is, that all his efforts, severe or gentle, are unavailing to pacify the fool.

data.verse_text: Men of bloodshed hate a blameless man, but the upright care for his life.
data.commentary_text:
bloodthirsty--(Compare Margin), murderers (Psa 5:6; Psa 26:9). 

hate, &c.-- (Pro 1:11; Gen 3:4). 

seek . . . soul--that is, to preserve it.

data.verse_text: A fool vents all his anger, but a wise man holds it back.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 12:16; Pro 16:32). 

mind--or, "spirit," for anger or any ill passion which the righteous restrain.

data.verse_text: If a ruler listens to lies, all his officials will be wicked.
data.commentary_text: His servants imitate him.

data.verse_text: The poor man and the oppressor have this in common: The LORD gives light to the eyes of both.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 22:2). 

deceitful man--literally, "man of vexations," an exactor. 

the Lord . . . their eyes--sustains their lives (Sa1 14:27; Psa 13:3); that is, both depend on Him, and He will do justice.

data.verse_text: A king who judges the poor with fairness— his throne will be established forever.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 20:28; Pro 25:5). Such is the character of the King of kings (Psa 72:4, Psa 72:12).

data.verse_text: A rod of correction imparts wisdom, but a child left to himself disgraces his mother.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 13:24; Pro 23:13).

data.verse_text: When the wicked thrive, rebellion increases; but the righteous will see their downfall.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 29:2, Pro 29:12; Psa 12:1-8). 

shall see . . . fall--and triumph in it (Psa 37:34-38; Psa 58:10-11).

data.verse_text: Discipline your son, and he will give you rest; he will bring delight to your soul.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 29:3, Pro 29:15; Pro 19:18). 

give thee rest--peace and quiet (compare Pro 29:9).

data.verse_text: Where there is no vision, the people cast off restraint; but blessed is he who keeps the Law.
data.commentary_text:
no vision--instruction in God's truth, which was by prophets, through visions (Sa1 3:1). 

people perish--(Compare Margin), are deprived of moral restraints. 

keepeth the law--has, and observes, instruction (Pro 14:11, Pro 14:34; Psa 19:11).

data.verse_text: A servant cannot be corrected by words alone; though he understands, he will not respond.
data.commentary_text:
A servant--who lacks good principle. 

corrected--or discovered. 

will not answer--that is, will not obey.

data.verse_text: Do you see a man who speaks in haste? There is more hope for a fool than for him.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 21:5). 

hasty in . . . words?--implying self-conceit (Pro 26:12).

data.verse_text: A servant pampered from his youth will bring grief in the end.
data.commentary_text: become his son--assume the place and privileges of one.

data.verse_text: An angry man stirs up dissension, and a hot-tempered man abounds in transgression.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Pro 15:18). Such are delighted by discord and violence.

data.verse_text: A man’s pride will bring him low, but a humble spirit will obtain honor.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 16:18; Pro 18:12). 

honour . . . spirit--or, "such shall lay hold on honor" (Pro 11:16).

data.verse_text: A partner to a thief hates his own soul; he receives the oath, but does not testify.
data.commentary_text:
hateth . . . soul--(Compare Pro 8:36). 

heareth cursing-- (Lev 5:1), risks the punishment, rather than reveal truth.

data.verse_text: The fear of man is a snare, but whoever trusts in the LORD is set securely on high.
data.commentary_text:
The fear . . . snare--involves men in difficulty (compare Pro 29:6). 

shall be safe--(Compare Margin; Pro 18:10).

data.verse_text: Many seek the ruler’s favor, but a man receives justice from the LORD.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Margin; Psa 27:8). God alone will and can do exact justice.

data.verse_text: An unjust man is detestable to the righteous, and one whose way is upright is detestable to the wicked.
data.commentary_text:
(Compare Pro 3:32). On last clause, compare Pro 29:16; Psa 37:12.




Next: Proverbs Chapter 30

data.verse_text: These are the words of Agur son of Jakeh—the burden that this man declared to Ithiel: “I am weary, O God, and worn out.
data.commentary_text:
(Pro. 30:1-33)

This is the title of this chapter (see Introduction). 

the prophecy--literally, "the burden" (compare Isa 13:1; Zac 9:1), used for any divine instruction; not necessarily a prediction, which was only a kind of prophecy (Ch1 15:27, "a song"). Prophets were inspired men, who spoke for God to man, or for man to God (Gen 20:7; Exo 7:14-16). Such, also, were the New Testament prophets. In a general sense, Gad, Nathan, and others were such, who were divine teachers, though we do not learn that they ever predicted. 

the man spake--literally, "the saying of the man"; an expression used to denote any solemn and important announcement (compare Sa2 23:1; Psa 36:1; Psa 110:1; Isa 1:24, &c.). Ithiel and Ucal were perhaps pupils.

data.verse_text: Surely I am the most ignorant of men, and I lack the understanding of a man. I have not learned wisdom, and I have no knowledge of the Holy One. Who has ascended to heaven and come down? Who has gathered the wind in His hands? Who has bound up the waters in His cloak? Who has established all the ends of the earth? What is His name, and what is the name of His Son— surely you know!
data.commentary_text: brutish--stupid, a strong term to denote his lowly self-estimation; or he may speak of such as his natural condition, as contrasted with God's all-seeing comprehensive knowledge and almighty power. The questions of this clause emphatically deny the attributes mentioned to be those of any creature, thus impressively strengthening the implied reference of the former to God (compare Deu 30:12-14; Isa 40:12; Eph 4:8).

data.verse_text: Every word of God is flawless; He is a shield to those who take refuge in Him.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Psa 12:6; Psa 119:140).

data.verse_text: Do not add to His words, lest He rebuke you and prove you a liar.
data.commentary_text:
Add . . . words--implying that his sole reliance was on God's all-sufficient teaching. 

reprove thee--or, "convict thee"--and so the falsehood will appear.

data.verse_text: Two things I ask of You— do not refuse me before I die:
data.commentary_text: A prayer for exemption from wickedness, and the extremes of poverty and riches, the two things mentioned. Contentment is implied as desired.

data.verse_text: Keep falsehood and deceitful words far from me. Give me neither poverty nor riches; feed me with the bread that is my portion.
data.commentary_text: vanity--all sorts of sinful acts (Job 11:11; Isa 5:18).

data.verse_text: Otherwise, I may have too much and deny You, saying, ‘Who is the LORD?’ Or I may become poor and steal, profaning the name of my God.
data.commentary_text:
be full . . . deny--that is, puffed up by the pride of prosperity. 

take the name . . . vain--This is not (Hebrew) the form (compare Exo 20:7), but "take" rather denotes laying violent hold on any thing; that is, lest I assail God's name or attributes, as justice, mercy, &c., which the poor are tempted to do.

data.verse_text: Do not slander a servant to his master, or he will curse you, and you will bear the guilt.
data.commentary_text:
Accuse not--Slander not (Psa 10:7). 

curse . . . guilty--lest, however lowly, he be exasperated to turn on thee, and your guilt be made to appear.

data.verse_text: There is a generation of those who curse their fathers and do not bless their mothers. There is a generation of those who are pure in their own eyes and yet unwashed of their filth. There is a generation—how haughty are their eyes and pretentious are their glances— there is a generation whose teeth are swords and whose jaws are knives, devouring the oppressed from the earth and the needy from among men.
data.commentary_text: Four kinds of hateful persons--(1) graceless children, (2) hypocrites, (3) the proud, (4) cruel oppressors (compare on Pro 30:14; Psa 14:4; Psa 52:2) --are now illustrated; (1) Pro 30:15-16, the insatiability of prodigal children and their fate; (2) Pro 30:17, hypocrisy, or the concealment of real character; (3 and 4) Pro 30:18-20, various examples of pride and oppression.

data.verse_text: The leech has two daughters: Give and Give. There are three things that are never satisfied, four that never say, ‘Enough!’: Sheol, the barren womb, land never satisfied with water, and fire that never says, ‘Enough!’
data.commentary_text:
horse leech--supposed by some to be the vampire (a fabulous creature), as being literally insatiable; but the other subjects mentioned must be taken as this, comparatively insatiable. The use of a fabulous creature agreeably to popular notions is not inconsistent with inspiration. 

There are three . . . yea, four--(Compare Pro 6:16).

data.verse_text: As for the eye that mocks a father and scorns obedience to a mother, may the ravens of the valley pluck it out and young vultures devour it.
data.commentary_text:
The eye--for the person, with reference to the use of the organ to express mockery and contempt, and also as that by which punishment is received. 

the ravens . . . eagles . . . eat--either as dying unnaturally, or being left unburied, or both.

data.verse_text: There are three things too wonderful for me, four that I cannot understand: the way of an eagle in the sky, the way of a snake on a rock, the way of a ship at sea, and the way of a man with a maiden.
data.commentary_text: Hypocrisy is illustrated by four examples of the concealment of all methods or traces of action, and a pertinent example of double dealing in actual vice is added, that is, the adulterous woman.

data.verse_text: This is the way of an adulteress: She eats and wipes her mouth and says, ‘I have done nothing wrong.’
data.commentary_text: she eateth . . . mouth--that is, she hides the evidences of her shame and professes innocence.

data.verse_text: Under three things the earth trembles, under four it cannot bear up: a servant who becomes king, a fool who is filled with food,
data.commentary_text: Pride and cruelty, the undue exaltation of those unfit to hold power, produce those vices which disquiet society (compare Pro 19:10; Pro 28:3).

data.verse_text: an unloved woman who marries, and a maidservant who supplants her mistress.
data.commentary_text: heir . . . mistress--that is, takes her place as a wife (Gen 16:4).

data.verse_text: Four things on earth are small, yet they are exceedingly wise: The ants are creatures of little strength, yet they store up their food in the summer;
data.commentary_text: These verses provide two classes of apt illustrations of various aspects of the moral world, which the reader is left to apply. By the first (Pro 30:25-28), diligence and providence are commended; the success of these insignificant animals being due to their instinctive sagacity and activity, rather than strength. The other class (Pro 30:30-31) provides similes for whatever is majestic or comely, uniting efficiency with gracefulness.

data.verse_text: the rock badgers are creatures of little power, yet they make their homes in the rocks; the locusts have no king, yet they all advance in formation;
data.commentary_text: conies--mountain mice, or rabbits.

data.verse_text: and the lizard can be caught in one’s hands, yet it is found in the palaces of kings. There are three things that are stately in their stride, and four that are impressive in their walk: a lion, mighty among beasts, refusing to retreat before anything; a strutting rooster; a he-goat; and a king with his army around him.
data.commentary_text:
spider--tolerated, even in palaces, to destroy flies. 

taketh . . . hands--or, uses with activity the limbs provided for taking prey.

data.verse_text: If you have foolishly exalted yourself or if you have plotted evil, put your hand over your mouth.
data.commentary_text:
As none can hope, successfully, to resist such a king, suppress even the thought of an attempt. 

lay . . . hand upon thy mouth--"lay" is well supplied (Jdg 18:19; Job 29:9; Job 40:4).

data.verse_text: For as the churning of milk yields butter, and the twisting of the nose draws blood, so the stirring of anger brings forth strife.”
data.commentary_text:
That is, strife--or other ills, as surely arise from devising evil as natural effects from natural causes.




Next: Proverbs Chapter 31

data.verse_text: These are the words of King Lemuel—the burden that his mother taught him:
data.commentary_text:
(Pro. 31:1-31)

On the title of this, the sixth part of the book, see Introduction. 

prophecy--(See on Pro 30:1).

data.verse_text: What shall I say, O my son? What, O son of my womb? What, O son of my vows?
data.commentary_text:
What, my son?--that is, What shall I say? Repetitions denote earnestness. 

son of my womb--as our phrase, "my own son," a term of special affection. 

son of my vows--as one dedicated to God; so the word "Lemuel" may mean.

data.verse_text: Do not spend your strength on women or your vigor on those who ruin kings.
data.commentary_text:
Succinct but solemn warnings against vices to which kings are peculiarly tempted, as carnal pleasures and oppressive and unrighteous government are used to sustain sensual indulgence. 

strength--mental and bodily resources for health and comfort. 

thy ways--or course of life. 

to that . . . kings--literally, "to the destroying of kings," avoid destructive pleasures (compare Pro 5:9; Pro 7:22, Pro 7:27; Hos 4:11).

data.verse_text: It is not for kings, O Lemuel, it is not for kings to drink wine, or for rulers to crave strong drink, lest they drink and forget what is decreed, depriving all the oppressed of justice.
data.commentary_text:
Stimulants enfeeble reason, pervert the heart, and do not suit rulers, who need clear and steady minds, and well-governed affections (compare Pro 20:1; Pro 22:29). 

pervert . . . afflicted--They give unrighteous decisions against the poor.

data.verse_text: Give strong drink to one who is perishing, and wine to the bitter in soul. Let him drink and forget his poverty, and remember his misery no more.
data.commentary_text: The proper use of such drinks is to restore tone to feeble bodies and depressed minds (compare Psa 104:15).

data.verse_text: Open your mouth for those with no voice, for the cause of all the dispossessed. Open your mouth, judge righteously, and defend the cause of the poor and needy.
data.commentary_text:
Open . . . cause--Plead for those who cannot plead for themselves, as the orphan, stranger, &c. (compare Psa 72:12; Isa 1:17). 

appointed to destruction--who are otherwise ruined by their oppressors (compare Pro 29:14, Pro 29:16).

data.verse_text: A wife of noble character, who can find? She is far more precious than rubies.
data.commentary_text:
This exquisite picture of a truly lovely wife is conceived and drawn in accordance with the customs of Eastern nations, but its moral teachings suit all climes. In Hebrew the verses begin with the letters of the Hebrew alphabet in order (compare Introduction to Poetical Books). 

Who . . . woman--The question implies that such are rare, though not entirely wanting (compare Pro 18:22; Pro 19:14). 

virtuous--literally, "of strength," that is, moral courage (compare Pro 12:4; Rut 3:11). 

her price, &c.--(compare Pro 3:15).

data.verse_text: The heart of her husband trusts in her, and he lacks nothing of value.
data.commentary_text:
heart . . . trust in her--He relies on her prudence and skill. 

no need of spoil--does not lack profit or gain, especially, that obtained by the risk of war.

data.verse_text: She brings him good and not harm all the days of her life.
data.commentary_text: do . . . good--contribute good to him.

data.verse_text: She selects wool and flax and works with eager hands. She is like the merchant ships, bringing her food from afar.
data.commentary_text: Ancient women of rank thus wrought with their hands; and such, indeed, were the customs of Western women a few centuries since. In the East also, the fabrics were articles of merchandise.

data.verse_text: She rises while it is still night to provide food for her household and portions for her maidservants.
data.commentary_text: She diligently attends to expending as well as gathering wealth;

data.verse_text: She appraises a field and buys it; from her earnings she plants a vineyard.
data.commentary_text: and hence has means to purchase property.

data.verse_text: She girds herself with strength and shows that her arms are strong. She sees that her gain is good, and her lamp is not extinguished at night.
data.commentary_text: To energy she adds a watchfulness in bargains, and a protracted and painful industry. The last clause may figuratively denote that her prosperity (compare Pro 24:20) is not short lived.

data.verse_text: She stretches out her hands to the distaff and grasps the spindle with her fingers.
data.commentary_text: No work, however mean, if honest, is disdained.

data.verse_text: She opens her arms to the poor and reaches out her hands to the needy.
data.commentary_text: Industry enables her to be charitable.

data.verse_text: When it snows, she has no fear for her household, for they are all clothed in scarlet.
data.commentary_text: scarlet--or, "purple," by reason of the dyes used, the best fabrics; as a matter of taste also; the color suits cold.

data.verse_text: She makes coverings for her bed; her clothing is fine linen and purple.
data.commentary_text:
coverings of tapestry--or, "coverlets," that is, for beds. 

silk--or, "linen" (compare Exo 26:1; Exo 27:9) 

and purple--that is, the most costly goods.

data.verse_text: Her husband is known at the city gate, where he sits among the elders of the land.
data.commentary_text: in the gates--(compare Pro 22:22). His domestic comfort promotes his advancement in public dignity.

data.verse_text: She makes linen garments and sells them; she delivers sashes to the merchants.
data.commentary_text:
fine linen--or, "linen shirts," or the material for them. 

girdles--were often costly and highly valued (Sa2 18:11). 

delivereth--or, "giveth as a present" or "to sell."

data.verse_text: Strength and honor are her clothing, and she can laugh at the days to come.
data.commentary_text:
Strength and honour--Strong and beautiful is her clothing; or, figuratively, for moral character, vigorous and honorable. 

shall rejoice . . . come--in confidence of certain maintenance.

data.verse_text: She opens her mouth with wisdom, and faithful instruction is on her tongue.
data.commentary_text: Her conversation is wise and gentle.

data.verse_text: She watches over the affairs of her household and does not eat the bread of idleness.
data.commentary_text: (Compare Ti1 5:14; Tit 2:5). She adds to her example a wise management of those under her control.

data.verse_text: Her children rise up and call her blessed; her husband praises her as well:
data.commentary_text: She is honored by those who best know her.

data.verse_text: “Many daughters have done noble things, but you surpass them all!”
data.commentary_text:
The words are those of her husband, praising her. 

virtuously--(Compare Pro 31:10).

data.verse_text: Charm is deceptive and beauty is fleeting, but a woman who fears the LORD is to be praised.
data.commentary_text:
Favour--or, "Grace" of personal manner. 

beauty--of face, or form (compare Pro 11:22). True piety alone commands permanent respect and affection (Pe1 3:3).

data.verse_text: Give her the fruit of her hands, and let her works praise her at the gates.
data.commentary_text:
The result of her labor is her best eulogy. Nothing can add to the simple beauty of this admirable portrait. On the measure of its realization in the daughters of our own day rest untold results, in the domestic, and, therefore, the civil and religious, welfare of the people.
